translation: Ta, Seen, Meem
commentary:
طسٓمٓ

These opening letters belong to the category of الحروف المقطعات - the disjointed letters that appear at the beginning of certain surahs. A pattern emerges when examining surahs that begin with these mysterious letters: they frequently feature the accounts of messengers, particularly Musa (AS), and often include mention of miraculous signs, especially the staff that transformed into a serpent.

translation: These are the verses of the clear Book
commentary:
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ

These are the miraculous signs of the clear and clarifying book. The choice of the demonstrative pronoun تِلْكَ ("those") rather than هَذِهِ ("these") carries profound meaning. The word "those" points to something distant, not immediately present. Allah ﷻ is establishing that these words the Prophet ﷺ recites are not his own creation, but rather a transcript being broadcast from an original book in Allah's possession - the preserved tablet, far above the heavens.

The term الْكِتَـٰبِ refers to something written, carved, or etched - not merely an oral tradition. This is particularly striking because this is a Makkan surah, revealed before the Quran existed in compiled book form that the companions could physically see. Yet Allah ﷻ uses the term "book" to emphasize its divine, preserved nature. The word الْمُبِينِ carries a dual meaning: the book itself is clear in its message, and it also clarifies and makes things clear for those who receive it.

translation: Perhaps, [O Muhammad], you would kill yourself with grief that they will not be believers
commentary:
لَعَلَّكَ بَـٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ

Is it possible that you are going to grieve and rage yourself to death because they will not become believers? The word بَـٰخِعٌ describes someone consumed by overwhelming grief and anger to the point of self-destruction. Modern medicine recognizes how stress, depression, and intense emotional turmoil can literally kill a person - the heart patient advised to avoid stressful situations, the devastating effects of chronic anxiety on the body.

Allah ﷻ addresses His beloved Prophet ﷺ with profound compassion: Are you going to kill yourself with worry over their rejection? The placement of this ayah is masterful. The previous ayah established that the Book itself is perfectly clear - there is nothing deficient in the message. If people still choose not to believe despite receiving such clear guidance, the Prophet ﷺ need not blame himself or think that somehow the message wasn't sufficient. The responsibility for rejection lies with the rejecters, not with the clarity of the revelation or the sincerity of the messenger.

This ayah sets up the central theme that will permeate the entire surah: What is enough? What constitutes sufficient evidence? Allah ﷻ will demonstrate through multiple prophetic accounts that He always provides enough proof, but for certain categories of people, nothing will ever be enough.

translation: If We willed, We could send down to them from the sky a sign for which their necks would remain humbled
commentary:
إِن نَّشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ءَايَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْنَـٰقُهُمْ لَهَا خَـٰضِعِينَ

If We had wanted, We would have sent them a miraculous sign from the sky, and their necks would have remained before it in humble submission. The Quraysh constantly demanded physical miracles - bring angels down from the sky, show us something spectacular that will force us to believe. Allah ﷻ responds that if He truly wanted to compel belief through an overwhelming supernatural display, He could easily do so. He could send something so undeniable that their necks would hang low in forced humility, with no choice but to submit.

But here's the profound mercy embedded in this statement: Allah ﷻ chooses not to do this. Why? Because previous nations received exactly such overwhelming signs, and when they still rejected them, they were immediately destroyed. The fact that Allah ﷻ withholds such a sign from the Quraysh is actually an act of mercy, giving them more time, more opportunities, more chances to come to faith on their own terms rather than through compulsion followed by swift annihilation.

The imagery of necks bowing is particularly powerful - it suggests a physical humiliation, a forced submission that bypasses the heart. This is not the kind of belief Allah ﷻ wants from His creation.

translation: And no revelation comes to them anew from the Most Merciful except that they turn away from it
commentary:
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ

And not a single reminder from Ar-Rahman comes to them - something fresh and renewed - except that they turn away from it. The choice to use the name الرَّحْمَـٰنِ here is deliberate and connects beautifully to the previous surah, which opened with this same divine name. Allah ﷻ is not sending them the same argument repeatedly; rather, He provides مُحْدَثٍ - something new, fresh, a different approach, a novel line of reasoning.

Consider the analogy of a teacher with a struggling student. If a student says "I don't understand," a good teacher will try explaining the concept a different way. When the student still doesn't get it, the teacher tries yet another approach, and another, and another. Each time, the same essential lesson, but presented through different angles, different examples, different methodologies. How many times can a teacher do this before frustration sets in?

The Quran itself embodies this divine pedagogy. Two-thirds of it could be summarized simply: Allah is One, worship Him alone; previous nations were destroyed for rejecting this message; believe in the messengers; the real accountability comes in the afterlife. This is not complicated theology. Yet Allah ﷻ presents these fundamental truths through hundreds of different stories, arguments, examples, and perspectives. The fact that الرَّحْمَـٰنِ (the Exceedingly Merciful) continues to offer fresh approaches despite persistent rejection demonstrates His infinite patience and mercy.

The ayah concludes with their predictable response: إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ (they were deliberate in turning away from it). This is not passive ignorance; it's active, willful rejection.

translation: For they have already denied, but there will come to them the news of that which they used to ridicule
commentary:
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

They have already denied, so soon the news - the realities - of whatever they've been ridiculing will come to them. This is a statement of inevitability. They mock the message, they ridicule the warnings, they make jokes about resurrection and accountability. But the punchline will be theirs when those very realities they mocked manifest before them. The word أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ refers to important news, significant information, real events - not abstract theories but concrete realities that will overtake them.

translation: Did they not look at the earth - how much We have produced therein from every noble kind
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ

Haven't they looked at the earth - how many kinds of noble, gracious plants We have caused to grow from it, all in pairs? Allah ﷻ now redirects their attention. They demand spectacular signs from the sky, but they've ignored the spectacular signs under their feet. The earth itself is a miracle - the diversity of plant life, the precision of botanical systems, the beauty and nobility of creation.

Two qualities are emphasized: the plants come in أَزْوَاجٍ (pairs) and they are كَرِيمٍ (noble, gracious, generous). Everything Allah ﷻ creates comes in pairs: male and female, body and soul, this life and the next, sun and moon, day and night, the messenger and the message. This pairing is itself a sign pointing to deeper truths. You are not just a body - you have a soul. This is not the only life - there is an afterlife. You cannot live on material knowledge alone - you need spiritual guidance as well.

Even before revelation descended, these signs in nature were calling people to reflection. Disbelieving in revelation is actually the second act of denial; the first was refusing to contemplate the creation all around them. If someone were already a person of reflection on the natural world and Allah's creative power, the transition to accepting revelation would be natural and easy.

translation: Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

Indeed, in that there is certainly a miraculous sign. But most of them are not going to be believers. Even in the observable signs of creation, there is an ayah - a miraculous sign that points to the Creator. Yet the ayah concludes with a sobering reality: most people will not believe. This is not pessimism but realism, preparing the Prophet ﷺ and the believers for the long road ahead.

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

And indeed, your Master is truly Al-Aziz, Ar-Rahim - the Possessor of Might, the Merciful. This combination of divine names appears repeatedly throughout this surah in a way that is relatively rare elsewhere in the Quran. Usually we find Al-Aziz paired with Al-Hakim (the Wise), suggesting that those who possess authority don't necessarily possess wisdom - but Allah ﷻ has both.

Here, Al-Aziz is paired with Ar-Rahim. Allah ﷻ has the absolute authority to fulfill their demands for spectacular miracles. He has the power to punish these obnoxious rejectors immediately. Yet despite possessing this ultimate authority, He continues to show mercy - offering them time, sending fresh arguments, providing opportunity after opportunity for them to turn to the truth. This pairing of attributes frames our understanding of everything that follows in the surah.

translation: And [mention] when your Lord called Moses, [saying], "Go to the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَإِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبُّكَ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱئْتِ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

And when your Master called out to Musa: "Go to the wrongdoing people." The account of Musa (AS) begins abruptly, without the elaborate setup found in other surahs. There's no mention of the journey to the mountain, no description of telling his family to wait while he investigates the fire, no detailed narrative of the burning bush. All of that has been covered in previous surahs - this is, as it were, "Season Two" of the story of Musa (AS).

The Quran's genius is evident in how it tells the same stories differently across multiple surahs, each time highlighting different aspects, different lessons, different angles. The Sahaba listening to this surah would immediately make connections - they're hearing echoes of stories they already know, but with new details, fresh emphasis, deeper insights.

Musa (AS) is commanded to go to "the wrongdoing people." The parallel to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is unmistakable. He too has been called to deliver the message to wrongdoers. The Sahaba listening would hear this and think: if Musa (AS) faced this, we're not alone in this struggle.

translation: The people of Pharaoh. Will they not fear Allah
commentary:
قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ أَلَا يَتَّقُونَ

The nation of Fir'awn - will they not have taqwa? Will they not protect themselves? The command is specific: not just any wrongdoing nation, but the nation of Fir'awn - the very people Musa (AS) fled from, the nation that has a warrant out for his arrest. This mirrors a pattern we see throughout the Quran: messengers are sent to capitals, to centers of power, not to every small town and village. The message must be delivered where it will have the greatest impact, where power is concentrated.

The question "will they not have taqwa?" is profound. Taqwa means to protect oneself - in this context, to protect oneself from destruction by submitting to the truth. But as we'll see, those in power rarely feel they need protection; they believe they are the protectors.

translation: He said, "My Lord, indeed I fear that they will deny me
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ

He said: "My Master, I am truly afraid that they will call me a liar." Musa (AS) voices his first concern, which directly mirrors what was said at the beginning of this surah about the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The opening ayahs addressed the Prophet's ﷺ grief that people wouldn't believe; now Musa (AS) expresses the same fear. The message is clear: You are not the first messenger to worry about rejection. Even Musa (AS), who spoke directly to Allah ﷻ, had the same concerns.

The verb كَذَّبَ is significant - it means to call someone a liar or to accuse them of lying. Musa (AS) fears they will either claim he's personally lying or that his message is false. Either way, it's a devastating accusation for someone delivering the truth.

translation: And that my breast will tighten and my tongue will not be fluent, so send for Aaron
commentary:
وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِى وَلَا يَنطَلِقُ لِسَانِى فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَـٰرُونَ

And my chest becomes tight, and my tongue doesn't move freely, so send word to Harun. This ayah reveals Musa's (AS) second concern, which is deeply human and relatable. When someone falsely accuses you, when they call your truth a lie, what happens physiologically? Your blood pressure rises, your chest constricts, you feel choked, you struggle to articulate clearly.

This is universal human experience. When we get intensely angry or frustrated, most of us lose our eloquence. Even people who are normally well-spoken find themselves tongue-tied when emotions run high. This is why in debates, one of the most effective tactics is to make your opponent angry - once they're emotional, they lose their train of thought and their arguments fall apart.

Musa (AS) knows himself. He knows he has a temper, and he knows that when he gets angry, his speech impediment worsens. He can already anticipate what will happen: they'll insult him, he'll get frustrated, his chest will tighten, his tongue will freeze, and they'll mock him further for his inability to speak. It's a vicious cycle he wants to avoid.

His solution: فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَـٰرُونَ (so send revelation to Harun). Notice the precise wording - he doesn't say "make Harun a messenger" or "can I bring Harun with me?" He says "send to Harun" - meaning, send him the same message You're sending me, give him the same mission, so when I tell him to come with me, he already understands his role. He's already on the same page. I won't have to explain everything and convince him; he'll already know this is from You.

Harun (AS) serves multiple purposes: he's backup in case something happens to Musa (AS), but more importantly, he's the one who will keep Musa (AS) calm, who will speak when Musa's (AS) tongue freezes, who will complement his brother's weaknesses with his own strengths.

translation: And they have upon me a [claim due to] sin, so I fear that they will kill me
commentary:
وَلَهُمْ عَلَىَّ ذَنۢبٌ فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ

And they have a crime listed against me, so I fear they will kill me. The third concern is about his physical safety, but notice the priority - Musa (AS) mentions this last, not first. His primary worry is about the mission: will they reject the message? Will I be able to deliver it properly? Only then does he mention: Oh, and by the way, they might execute me.

This reveals the character of the prophets - the mission matters more than their personal safety. Their own lives are secondary to ensuring the message gets delivered clearly.

The word ذَنۢبٌ is carefully chosen. It refers to something embarrassing, something with bad consequences, not necessarily something evil in itself. The act - defending someone who was being oppressed - may have been justifiable, but the outcome was terrible: someone died. That makes it a dhanb, something he's ashamed of, something with serious repercussions.

translation: [Allah] said, "No. Go both of you with Our signs; indeed, We are with you, listening
commentary:
قَالَ كَلَّا ۖ فَٱذْهَبَا بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ

He said: "Not at all. So both of you go with Our miraculous signs. Indeed, We are with you, listening carefully." Allah's ﷻ response is stunning in its brevity and completeness. One word - كَلَّا ("not at all") - addresses all of Musa's (AS) concerns simultaneously. Don't worry about them calling you a liar - not your problem. Don't worry about your chest getting tight or your tongue freezing - I'll handle it. Don't worry about being killed - you'll be protected.

Then, without Musa (AS) even asking for it, Allah ﷻ adds something extra: فَٱذْهَبَا (so both of you go). The dual form confirms that Harun (AS) will accompany him, and more than that - بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ (with Our signs). Not just the two signs given on the mountain (the staff and the hand), but multiple signs, equipped with everything they'll need throughout the entire mission, right up to the parting of the sea.

The ayah concludes with beautiful reassurance: إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ (We are with you, listening carefully). Earlier in Surah Ta-Ha, Allah ﷻ said إِنَّنِى مَعَكُمَآ أَسْمَعُ وَأَرَىٰ (I am with you both, hearing and seeing). Here the emphasis shifts to listening - مُّسْتَمِعُونَ suggests intense, careful attention to what will be said. This alerts us that the conversation about to be recorded carries special significance. If Allah ﷻ is telling us He listened carefully to this dialogue, we should listen carefully too.

translation: Go to Pharaoh and say, 'We are the messengers of the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَأْتِيَا فِرْعَوْنَ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

So approach Fir'awn and say: "We are the messenger of the Master of all nations and peoples." The verb فَأْتِيَا (approach) is significant. It comes from أَتَى , which means to come or go somewhere without difficulty, casually. This is contrasted with جَاءَ, which suggests overcoming obstacles, a difficult journey, something grand and momentous.

Where does Fir'awn live? In a fortified palace, surrounded by elite guards, security checkpoints, layers of protection. Nobody just "walks in" to see Fir'awn. Yet Allah ﷻ uses the casual form: just go, just approach, as if the gates will simply open before you. And that's exactly what happened - the narrative skips all the obstacles and takes us straight into the throne room.

The instruction they're given contains powerful rhetoric: إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (We are the messenger of the Master of all worlds). Several elements demand attention:

First, the singular "messenger" despite the dual "we." This grammatical peculiarity emphasizes their unity - they cannot be separated, they function as one unit, one message delivered by one unified voice. When guards tried to separate them ("slaves can't enter"), this singular form gave them the argument to insist they must enter together.

Second, the word رَبِّ (Master) directly challenges Fir'awn's claim to divinity. You walk into the court of the man who declares "I am your supreme lord" and you say "Actually, we're here from the real Master" - meaning you are not. That's an insult punishable by immediate execution, yet it's the very first thing they're instructed to say.

Third, the phrase ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (all worlds, all peoples, all nations) leaves no ambiguity. Master of what? Everything. Including you, including your kingdom, including your nation.

translation: [Commanded to say], "Send with us the Children of Israel
commentary:
أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ

"Send with us the Children of Israel." This is the demand: release your entire enslaved workforce. Consider the economic implications. Fir'awn's empire is built on free labor - hundreds of thousands of people working without wages, their only "salary" being minimal food to keep them alive and productive. They build his monuments, maintain his kingdom, sustain his power.

Why would he ever agree to let them go? The demand seems impossible from every rational angle. Yet this is what messengers do - they deliver the message regardless of how impossible it seems, regardless of the odds, regardless of the expected outcome.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "Did we not raise you among us as a child, and you remained among us for years of your life
commentary:
قَالَ أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينَا وَلِيدًا وَلَبِثْتَ فِينَا مِنْ عُمُرِكَ سِنِينَ

He said: "Did we not raise you among us as a child? And you stayed with us for many years of your life." Now we cut to Fir'awn's response, and the Quran's narrative genius shines. We're not told about the journey down the mountain, the 180-mile trek, the negotiations with guards at multiple checkpoints, the climb up palace floors, the waiting, the protocol. All of that is skipped. We go straight to the throne room, straight to the confrontation, because that's where our curiosity lies: How did Fir'awn respond? How did Musa (AS) not get killed immediately?

Fir'awn's response is masterful manipulation. Notice: Musa (AS) came with two clear demands - recognize us as messengers of Allah, and release Bani Israel. Fir'awn addresses neither. Instead, he changes the subject entirely, weaponizing emotion and guilt.

"Don't you remember when you were a baby here? When you used to run around in these halls? When you climbed on my lap? I raised you. I gave you a home, education, protection, the clothes on your back. I paid for everything. And this is how you speak to me? After everything I did for you?"

This is a textbook logical fallacy - the appeal to emotion through guilt. It's used everywhere: "How can you call yourself an American and say that? Don't you respect the sacrifices made for this country?" A daughter converts to Islam and her father says, "So you're accepting the religion of the enemy? You're better than your own mother now?"

The logic is twisted, but it's emotionally effective. It's meant to derail the conversation, to make the other person defensive, apologetic, backing down. Fir'awn is saying: I expect you to walk in here grateful, not confrontational. I expect "Hey, good to see you, can I have my old room back?" not these demands.

translation: And [then] you did your deed which you did, and you were of the ungrateful
commentary:
وَفَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ ٱلَّتِى فَعَلْتَ وَأَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

"And you did that deed of yours that you did, and you are among the ungrateful." Before Musa (AS) can even respond to the guilt trip, Fir'awn cuts him off with another blow. But notice the careful, calculated vagueness: "You did that thing that you did."

What thing? The murder. But Fir'awn doesn't spell it out. He's playing good cop, showing "mercy" by not explicitly stating the crime in front of his entire court. "I'm being so gracious, I'm not even going to say what you did, but you know what you did, right? Should I tell everyone? I'll tell everyone if you push me."

The word فَعْلَةَ (deed, action) is deliberately ambiguous, creating suspense for everyone listening. The courtiers are thinking: What is he talking about? What action?

Then Fir'awn adds: وَأَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (and you are among the ungrateful). The word كَـٰفِرِينَ carries dual meaning - those who deny/cover truth, and those who are ungrateful. Both applications work here: "You're denying what you did, aren't you? And after all I did for you, you're proving yourself ungrateful."

Musa (AS) has been hit with a derailment barrage. He came with two clear points to deliver. Now he's being made to feel guilty about his upbringing, he's being accused of a crime, he's being called ungrateful and a denier. There's a lot to respond to, a lot of misconceptions to clear up, a lot of ways this could go wrong.

This parallels exactly what Muslims face when trying to share Islam. We come with a message to deliver, and immediately we're derailed: "What about terrorism? What about women? What about this historical incident? Aren't you being ungrateful to the country that gave you opportunities?" Before we know it, we're defending, explaining, apologizing - and we've forgotten the original message we came to share.

translation: [Moses] said, "I did it, then, while I was of those astray
commentary:
قَالَ فَعَلْتُهَآ إِذًا وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ

He said: "I did it at that time, and I was among those who were misguided." This response demonstrates the brilliance and composure of Musa (AS). Despite Allah's ﷻ earlier prediction that he'd get flustered and tongue-tied, here his tongue works perfectly. He doesn't run from the most controversial issue - he addresses it head-on.

His strategy is masterful: First, he prioritizes. Fir'awn raised three issues - you lived here, you stayed many years, you committed murder. Which is the elephant in the room? Which is the one everyone is actually concerned about? The murder charge. Everything else is secondary. So Musa (AS) addresses that one first, directly, without evasion.

Second, he's smart about what he admits and what he doesn't. Notice Fir'awn never explicitly said "murder" - he just said "that thing you did." Why should Musa (AS) spell out the details and make himself look worse? If Fir'awn is holding back from being explicit, Musa (AS) does the same: "Yes, I did it." Did what? Left vague, just like Fir'awn left it vague.

Third, and most importantly, he takes full responsibility: وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (I was among those who were misguided, those who were lost). He doesn't say "Someone misled me." He doesn't say "It wasn't my fault." He doesn't blame Shaytan, circumstances, or anyone else. He owns it: I was lost, I was confused, I made a mistake.

This is profound. Later, in Surah Al-Qasas, we'll learn that when Musa (AS) spoke to Allah ﷻ about this incident, he said "This is from the work of Shaytan, he is a clear enemy that misguides." It's true that Shaytan does mislead. But then immediately after acknowledging Shaytan's role, Musa (AS) said "My Master, I have wronged myself, so forgive me." He blamed Shaytan, but he didn't use that to escape his own responsibility.

This is the balance we must learn: Yes, Shaytan is real and actively works to misguide us. But we cannot use that as an excuse to avoid accountability for our own choices. On the Day of Judgment, Shaytan will say "Don't blame me - you listened to me voluntarily. I didn't force you." The person of true character admits their faults. The criminal finds someone else to blame.

In Fir'awn's court, surrounded by hostile witnesses, facing potential execution, Musa (AS) demonstrates dignity and truth. He doesn't grovel, doesn't make excuses, doesn't shift blame. He simply states the facts: Yes, at that time in my life, I was lost and made a terrible mistake. This honesty and directness will set the stage for everything that follows in this remarkable confrontation.

translation: So I fled from you when I feared you. Then my Lord granted me wisdom and prophethood and appointed me [as one] of the messengers
commentary:
فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِى رَبِّى حُكْمًا وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

Musa (AS) continues his response to Fir'awn's accusations by acknowledging what happened while simultaneously redirecting the conversation back to his divine mission. When he says فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ (I fled from all of you), the use of "all of you" carries significant weight. He isn't addressing only Fir'awn, but the entire court – the generals, the chiefs, everyone present. This is deeply insulting in royal protocol, where speaking to anyone other than the king himself is considered contempt of court. Later in the narrative, we learn that when Musa (AS) was about to leave the city years ago, the chiefs had plotted to kill him. So his words carry an implicit message: I know which of you planned my death.

The phrase لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ (when I used to fear you) is particularly powerful. The past tense here indicates that this fear is ancient history. Musa (AS) is declaring that he no longer fears any of them. He once ran, but he's not running anymore – he has returned.

What gave him this transformation? فَوَهَبَ لِى رَبِّى حُكْمًا (My Master gave me wisdom and authority). Notice how he returns to the word "Master" – the one he introduced when he first walked into the court. This Master, his real Master, is the source of his newfound courage and conviction. The word حُكْمًا (hukman) encompasses both wisdom and the authority to judge, the firmness to stand before tyrants without trembling.

وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (And He made me from among the messengers) – Musa (AS) repeats his first point. Perhaps Fir'awn didn't hear it the first time. Despite the tangent about the killing, despite the attempt to make him feel guilty, Musa (AS) returns to his mission: I am a messenger from my Master. This isn't a social visit or a debate about past crimes. This is a divine commission.

translation: And is this a favor of which you remind me - that you have enslaved the Children of Israel
commentary:
وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَىَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ

Fir'awn tries another tangent: You lived here for many years. I raised you in this house. That is a huge favor I did for you. The word تَمُنُّهَا (you recount it) carries the meaning of rubbing a favor in someone's face, making them feel guilty about kindness shown to them. It's like someone saying, "Remember that time I helped you when you really needed it? Just in case you forgot how much better I am than you."

Musa (AS) responds with devastating logic: وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَىَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ – "And that is a favor you're rubbing in my face because you enslaved the Children of Israel?" The connection he makes is brilliant. You're saying you raised me in your house as if that's some great act of nobility. But what does that have to do with enslaving an entire nation? How are these two things equivalent? One person getting free housing versus half a million people in chains?

This isn't just a response – it's an insult to Fir'awn's intelligence. Between the lines, Musa (AS) is saying: That reasoning is absurd. That's stupid. How do you think that makes sense?

The first point was an insult to the king's authority by addressing others. The second point is an insult to the king's intellect. Fir'awn is being called out for faulty logic, and now he must deal with the actual issue he was trying to avoid: the enslavement of Bani Israel.

translation: Said Pharaoh, "And what is the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَا رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Unable to maintain his intellectual evasion, Fir'awn shifts to dismissiveness. Notice the word he chooses: مَا (what) instead of مَن (who). If he genuinely wanted to know about the Master of all nations, he would ask "Who is the Master?" But by asking "What Master?" he's treating the concept as absurd, as though this Master is a figment of Musa's imagination.

This is psychological warfare through language. When someone says in English, "What are you talking about?" they aren't seeking clarification – they're dismissing what you're saying as nonsense. That's exactly what Fir'awn is doing. He's trying to make the idea of Allah ﷻ seem ridiculous, unworthy of serious consideration.

The question وَمَا رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ carries this mocking tone: "What master of nations? What are you even talking about?" It's designed to throw Musa (AS) off balance and make him seem foolish before the court.

translation: [Moses] said, "The Lord of the heavens and earth and that between them, if you should be convinced
commentary:
قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ

Musa (AS) demonstrates remarkable composure. Instead of getting upset at the insulting manner in which Fir'awn spoke about Allah ﷻ, he pretends the "what" was actually "who" – as though Fir'awn had asked a genuine question. He responds: رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (The Master of the heavens and the earth).

This choice is strategic and devastating. The Egyptians worshipped the sun. The pharaohs believed themselves to be descendants of the sun god, making them gods of the earth. By mentioning the heavens first, Musa (AS) is declaring: My Master owns the very skies in which your supposed god lives. He owns the entire celestial realm. And since you believe yourselves to be descendants of that sun and therefore gods of the earth, know this: He owns the earth too. You are not gods – you never were.

وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ (and whatever is between them) – this phrase trivializes their sun god even further. The sun, which they worship with such reverence, is dismissed as merely something "between" the heavens and earth, one created thing among countless others.

Then Musa (AS) does something even bolder. He doesn't say إِن كُنتَ مُّوقِنًا (if you want to be convinced), addressing Fir'awn directly. Instead, he says إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (if all of you are seeking conviction), turning to the entire crowd. He's offering them يَقِين (yaqeen) – absolute certainty that removes all doubt. Death is yaqeen because no one can deny it. Musa (AS) is saying: If you want that level of unshakeable conviction, I have something to show you.

He hasn't revealed his miracles yet, but he's testing the waters, setting the stage. And by addressing the crowd instead of Fir'awn, he's ignoring the tyrant, treating him as someone not genuinely interested in an answer.

translation: [Pharaoh] said to those around him, "Do you not hear
commentary:
قَالَ لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥٓ أَلَا تَسْتَمِعُونَ

Fir'awn is now furious. He's been doubly insulted: first by not being addressed directly, and second by watching his own court become captivated by this messenger. Remember, Fir'awn is a dictator. People don't speak in front of him. People don't refute him. Employees don't give their opinions. Those who do often disappear. When you have a boss like that, you internally hope for the day someone finally stands up to him.

Even though these people officially regard Fir'awn as a god deserving ultimate respect, they haven't defended him once during this exchange. They haven't expressed outrage at the insults Musa (AS) has delivered. If they truly believed in Fir'awn's divinity, shouldn't they be offended on his behalf? Their silence is deafening.

Worse still, when Musa (AS) turns to them and says "if you're looking to be convinced," they actually look interested. They're engaged. They want to hear more. And Fir'awn sees all of this.

قَالَ لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥٓ (He said to those around him) – now he specifically addresses the generals, the chiefs, the inner circle. Everyone includes the insignificant members earlier, but now he needs to rally his most important supporters. أَلَا تَسْتَمِعُونَ (Didn't you hear what he said? Why aren't you offended?)

The tables are turning. At the beginning of this dialogue, Fir'awn sat elevated on his throne while Musa (AS) walked in disheveled from travel, carrying the label of murderer. But as this conversation continues, Fir'awn's position weakens. His stupidity is being exposed. His tangents are being turned back against him. His own crowd is more impressed with this supposed criminal than with their king. The power of clear speech is bringing a dictator down in his own court.

translation: [Moses] said, "Your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers
commentary:
قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

Musa (AS) sees Fir'awn's frustration but refuses to be distracted. The conversation hasn't actually moved forward – Fir'awn hasn't said anything of substance. So Musa (AS) continues answering the original question as though the interruption never happened.

رَبُّكُمْ (Your Master) – he emphasizes that this applies to everyone present, not just himself. The Master I'm talking about is the Master of all of you. Then he goes deeper: وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (and the Master of your most ancient ancestors).

This strikes at something the Egyptians held sacred: their ancestry. If there's one thing Egyptians took immense pride in, it was their lineage. They carved monuments, built pyramids, created elaborate records of their dynasties. They constructed massive structures by the water so that nations traveling past would witness the magnificence of the Egyptian kingdom. To this day, documentaries examine how great this civilization was. The American dollar bill carries the pyramid as a symbol of that impressive legacy.

Musa (AS) takes direct aim at this source of pride: Those ancestors you honor so much? Those kings who built your empire? They weren't masters. They were slaves to my Master. Your entire glorious history, all those dynasties you worship – they all belonged to Him, not to themselves.

At this point, Musa (AS) has completely taken ownership of the conversation. He's no longer the accused defendant. He's the one in control, dismantling Fir'awn's authority piece by piece.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "Indeed, your 'messenger' who has been sent to you is mad
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ

Fir'awn has run out of intellectual responses. He tried anger, insults, guilt trips, tangents – none of it worked. Now he moves to character assassination, the last resort of someone who has lost the argument.

إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ (Your messenger) – notice the word "your." Not "this messenger" or "the messenger" but your messenger, as though he's speaking to his own people saying: You've already accepted him. The way you're acting, it's like you believe he's been sent to you. What's wrong with you people?

This reveals something extraordinary. Before any miracles have been displayed – before the staff turns into a snake, before the hand glows white – Fir'awn already sees that his closest advisors are so convinced by Musa (AS) that he feels compelled to remind them who they should be loyal to.

ٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ (the one who has apparently been sent to you) – he still can't bring himself to acknowledge that this messenger came primarily for him. Allah ﷻ told Musa (AS) to go to the "nation of Fir'awn," but Fir'awn refuses to accept that the message is for him first.

لَمَجْنُونٌ (he is surely insane) – the word majnoon indicates someone whose intellect has been overshadowed, someone possessed, someone who has lost their mind. It's an attempt to discredit everything Musa (AS) has said by attacking his mental state rather than addressing his arguments.

translation: [Moses] said, "Lord of the east and the west and that between them, if you were to reason
commentary:
قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

Musa (AS) continues as though he didn't even hear the insult. He's still answering the original question: What Master are you talking about?

رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ (The Master of the East and the West) – why is this significant to the Egyptian Empire? Because they worship the sun. Musa (AS) is declaring: My Master owns where your false god rises and sets. Your deity is confined to a path it must follow. My Master determines that path.

وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ (and whatever lies between them) – the land itself, which Fir'awn claims to rule, actually belongs to Allah ﷻ. This isn't your kingdom, Fir'awn. You don't own this land. My Master does.

إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (if you understand what I'm saying) – again, he addresses the crowd, not Fir'awn. The implication is powerful: If you get what I'm saying, you'll realize this guy is not really your king. Allah ﷻ is. It's almost an invitation to a coup: He's just one person. There are many of you. If you're with me...

This is why Fir'awn suddenly realizes the situation has become extremely serious. If Musa (AS) is allowed to continue speaking, a revolution might actually happen. They might turn against their king right here in the throne room.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "If you take a god other than me, I will surely place you among those imprisoned
commentary:
قَالَ لَئِنِ ٱتَّخَذْتَ إِلَـٰهًا غَيْرِى لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمَسْجُونِينَ

The moment Fir'awn calls for arrest, he publicly admits defeat. When a ruler has no words left to crush what someone is saying, he resorts to force. If this exchange were being broadcast, if this were an election debate, everyone watching would know: Fir'awn just got destroyed.

لَئِنِ ٱتَّخَذْتَ إِلَـٰهًا غَيْرِى (If you dare take a god other than me) – he phrases it as though this is about divine authority, but everyone knows it's about political power.

لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمَسْجُونِينَ (I will surely place you among those imprisoned) – there are already people in prison for the "crime" of believing in the God of Ya'qub (AS) and Isra'il (AS). There are already Muslims locked away for refusing to worship Fir'awn. This isn't an empty threat. It's state policy.

But by making this threat, Fir'awn reveals his weakness. A king is most dependent on the loyalty of his closest cabinet. These are the people he needs most, and these are the people he fears most. They're closest to the throne, which means they're most capable of taking it. Kings throughout history have executed their own family members out of this fear. And right now, Fir'awn sees in their eyes that spark of doubt, that hint of shifting loyalty. So he threatens force because he has nothing else left.

translation: [Moses] said, "Even if I brought you proof manifest
commentary:
قَالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكَ بِشَىْءٍ مُّبِينٍ

Musa (AS) recognizes the situation. The time for speaking is nearly over. If he continues talking, he'll be arrested. His tongue has done its job – now it's time for what Allah ﷻ equipped him with that doesn't require speech.

أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكَ بِشَىْءٍ مُّبِينٍ (What if I brought you something absolutely clear?) – the word شَىْءٍ (something) is deliberately vague, but مُّبِينٍ (mubin) indicates it's grand, unmistakable, impossible to deny.

Musa (AS) came in with no luggage, no equipment, just a shepherd's staff. What "grand thing" could he possibly show? Fir'awn looks at him skeptically: What are you going to give me, your stick?

Actually, yes.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "Then bring it, if you should be of the truthful
commentary:
قَالَ فَأْتِ بِهِۦٓ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

فَأْتِ بِهِۦٓ (Then bring it) – Fir'awn thinks Musa (AS) is stalling, trying desperately to avoid arrest. He's already issued the order in his mind; he's just giving Musa (AS) one more moment to embarrass himself.

إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (if you are from among the truthful) – the sarcasm is evident. Come on, show me. What do you have? You're just desperate now.

Fir'awn thinks he's finally about to win. He's got nothing, Fir'awn tells himself. This is over. I've scored my first point.

translation: So [Moses] threw his staff, and suddenly it was a serpent manifest
commentary:
فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ

فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ (Then he threw his staff) – and immediately, without warning, without buildup, فَإِذَا (suddenly) it transformed.

ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (a massive, clearly visible python) – in other passages, we learned about its fangs. Here we learn about its size. This isn't a garden snake. This is a man-eating python, a creature of nightmares, appearing suddenly in an indoor throne room.

Imagine the chaos. I once taught a class where a mouse ran through the sisters' section. The reaction was immediate – screaming, panic, people climbing over each other, and it was just a mouse. Now imagine a massive python appearing out of nowhere in a royal court. The security guards with their spears, the generals in their decorated uniforms – everyone is terrified. They're climbing on chairs, hiding behind each other, frozen in fear.

The only terrifying entity in that court used to be Fir'awn. He commanded fear if not respect or loyalty. But Allah ﷻ just stripped that away. In an instant, something far more frightening appeared, and everyone – including Fir'awn – knows they're powerless against it.

Before this moment, all ears were on Musa (AS). Now all eyes are on what he brought. He owns their attention completely.

translation: And he drew out his hand; thereupon it was white for the observers
commentary:
وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ

In the middle of this chaos, while people are still reeling from the sight of the serpent, وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ (he drew out his hand).

فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ (and suddenly it was brilliantly white) – the word bayda indicates a luminous, pure white, radiating light. This isn't a skin condition or discoloration. This is supernatural brilliance.

لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (for those looking on) – everyone is staring. They can't look away. Some are still watching the snake. Others are now fixated on this glowing hand. What is happening? How is this possible?

The snake itself was white, we learn elsewhere, and now the hand matches it. The visual spectacle is overwhelming. People are asking each other: What is that? Why is his hand like that? Watch out for the snake! Don't get too close! The court has descended into utter chaos, and standing calm in the middle of it all is Musa (AS), the supposed criminal, now wielding the power of Allah ﷻ in undeniable display.

translation: [Pharaoh] said to the eminent ones around him, "Indeed, this is a learned magician
commentary:
قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُۥٓ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ

Fir'awn needs to regain control. The situation is spiraling. He calls out: قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُۥٓ (He said to the chiefs around him) – notice the specificity. Earlier he addressed everyone (لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥٓ), including insignificant court members. Now he addresses only الْمَلَإِ, the generals, the chieftains, the people whose loyalty he desperately needs.

Everyone, calm down. Relax. Let me explain what's happening here.

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ (This is surely an extremely knowledgeable magician) – he doesn't say "I think" or "perhaps." He says إِنَّ (indeed, without doubt). This guy is a powerful sorcerer. That's the official explanation. That's the story we're going with.

Later we'll learn this becomes state policy. When people talk about what they witnessed – and they will talk – the government line is: "extremely knowledgeable magician." No other explanation is permitted.

Fir'awn is already in damage control mode, calling an emergency security cabinet meeting right there in the throne room. He needs to unify his administration's messaging. In any government, if one member contradicts what the leader said, it destroys credibility for everyone. So he tells them: Get on the same page. This is what happened. We all agree on this. Understood?

But he knows there's doubt. One general might say, "Sir, I don't think that was magic." Fir'awn's response: "Did you not hear me? Extremely powerful magician. No doubt about it."

translation: He wants to drive you out of your land by his magic, so what do you advise
commentary:
يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِۦ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ

Now Fir'awn makes a calculated move. He needs to secure their loyalty, but he can't be heavy-handed about it. They've just watched someone more impressive than Fir'awn himself. They're already half-convinced. So he softens his approach and appeals to their self-interest.

يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم (He wants to drive you out from your land) – not "my throne" but your land. This is a threat to you. Your homes, your property, your status, your power – all of it is at risk. He frames it as a national security threat rather than a personal challenge to his authority.

بِسِحْرِهِۦ (by means of his magic) – and he's using this sorcery to manipulate you, to turn you against your own country.

فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ (So what do you command?) – this is remarkable. Fir'awn is asking their opinion. He, who never asks anyone anything, who executes people for speaking without permission, is now saying: What do you think we should do?

But notice the brilliance: he doesn't say "What do you command me to do?" His pride won't allow that. He says فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ, which can mean "what do you all command" or "what should be commanded" – deliberately ambiguous, avoiding direct acknowledgment that he's seeking their guidance.

This is a display of weakness. A king who asks for opinions reveals he doesn't have all the answers. And these generals, who have never been consulted in their lives, suddenly feel their own power growing. Some are clenching their fists thinking: After years of being ignored, today he's asking for my input.

translation: They said, "Postpone [the matter of] him and his brother and send among the cities gatherers
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَٱبْعَثْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ

The generals respond with their strategy: أَرْجِهْ (delay him) – the word comes from إرجاء, which has a unique meaning. It's like holding food in your teeth without swallowing it, or keeping something in your mouth without consuming it. Applied here, it means: Let him go, but keep an extremely close watch on him.

Why let him go? Because if they arrest Musa (AS) now, these same generals will use their lunch breaks to visit him in prison. "Let's go hear what else this man has to say. That was incredible." They're intrigued. They're impressed. Arresting him only makes him more appealing, more mysterious. But if they give him free reign and undermine him through propaganda, they can weaken his influence.

وَأَخَاهُ (and his brother too) – don't forget about Harun (AS). Keep tabs on both of them.

وَٱبْعَثْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (and send recruiters into all the cities) – Egypt controls multiple cities, each with its own school of magic, its own experts, its own traditions. Gather them all. Create an Olympic team of multinational magicians, the best from every region.

The generals are confident: This is what we do. Egypt is famous for magic. We have this under control.

translation: Who will bring you every learned, skilled magician
commentary:
يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ

يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ (They will bring you every knowledgeable sorcerer) – not just any magicians, but the elite, the سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ, those with deep expertise and years of experience.

The word سَحَّار is an intensive form, indicating someone who is exceptionally skilled. And عَلِيمٍ means knowledgeable, well-versed, expert. These aren't street performers. These are the masters of the craft.

The generals speak with certainty. This is an easy problem to solve. Magic is Egypt's specialty. Musa (AS) might have impressed us momentarily, but wait until our people show him what real skill looks like. They will easily bring you every kind of powerful magician. We've got this.

translation: So the magicians were assembled for the appointment of a well-known day
commentary:
فَجُمِعَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ لِمِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ

فَجُمِعَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ (So the magicians were gathered) – the passive voice here is significant. They were gathered, rounded up by the state. This wasn't a voluntary assembly. Soldiers went door to door, collecting the best magicians from across the empire.

لِمِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (for the appointed time of a well-known day) – the word مِيقَـٰتِ refers to both the appointed time and the appointed place. And مَّعْلُومٍ (well-known) is important because we already learned about this day in previous passages. It's their day of celebration, their Independence Day, the festival of Zeenah when all of Egypt comes together in national pride.

The reader of the Quran says: "A well-known day? Which day is that? Do I know it?" Yes, you do. It was mentioned before – the day of their parades, their festivities, their commemoration of national glory.

This choice is strategic. It's the day when national spirit is at its peak, when loyalty to Egypt and its rulers is most fervent. Fir'awn will use that patriotic energy to frame his struggle against Musa (AS) as Egypt's struggle. If you love your country, you're against Musa (AS). That's the message.

translation: And it was said to the people, "Will you congregate
commentary:
وَقِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّجْتَمِعُونَ

وَقِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ (And it was said to the people) – this wasn't a casual announcement. It was a mobilization. Soldiers went door to door, not just distributing information but ensuring compliance.

هَلْ أَنتُم مُّجْتَمِعُونَ (Will you gather?) – the phrasing makes it sound like a question, but it's really a command. Are you coming to the event or what? This isn't optional. The army is making sure everyone shows up. Bring your family. Be there.

Why this intense effort to gather crowds? Because in the time between the throne room incident and this public event, Musa (AS) hasn't been on vacation. He's not taking a break until September. He's doing exactly what he was sent to do: delivering the message of Allah ﷻ to the people. He's preaching among them, and word is spreading. We learned in other passages that he was actively calling people to truth during this period.

So Fir'awn needs everyone present for the public humiliation of Musa (AS). Why? Because everybody already knows some version of what happened. The security guards went home and told their wives. The wives told their friends. Egypt was talking. It was the trending topic. People had their own versions: "Musa (AS) walked in with a giant snake and Fir'awn nearly fainted," or "The snake wrapped around Fir'awn's neck," or "Musa (AS) slapped him across the face and said 'You're not God!'"

These rumors are devastating for Fir'awn. Every version makes him look weak, makes him look defeated. So he needs a public event where everyone witnesses Musa (AS) being destroyed, where the official narrative can overwrite all these stories.

translation: That we might follow the magicians if they are the predominant
commentary:
لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ ٱلسَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ

The people are told to say: لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ ٱلسَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (Perhaps we will follow the magicians if they are the ones who overcome).

On the surface, this means: If the magicians defeat Musa (AS), we'll follow them instead of him. That's the advertising campaign, the official talking point. People are saying: "Yeah, sure, if the magicians win, we'll stick with our side."

But there's something deeper happening. In public perception, Fir'awn has been embarrassed and humiliated. Who is now being presented as the heroes who will save the nation's dignity? Not Fir'awn himself, but the magicians. They are being marketed as the champions, the saviors, the defenders of Egyptian honor against this national threat.

The magicians are getting better publicity than Fir'awn. People are starting to talk: "These magicians are amazing. Maybe after they win, we won't even care so much about Fir'awn anymore. Maybe we'll have an administration of magicians." Everywhere the magicians go, people salute them, wish them luck, ask for autographs, treat them like celebrities.

Do you think the magicians heard these rumors? Absolutely. They heard people talking about how influential and important they were becoming. They felt the shift in power dynamics. And they realized: We have leverage.

translation: And when the magicians arrived, they said to Pharaoh, "Is there indeed for us a reward if we are the predominant
commentary:
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ قَالُوا۟ لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ

Picture the magicians in their preparation space, comparing notes. "So what are you getting paid?" one asks. "Paid? Soldiers burst into my house and dragged me here. I'm not getting paid," another responds. They look around. None of them are being compensated. They're essentially conscripted labor.

One of them says: "Should we ask for payment?" The others respond: "Are you crazy? You know what he does to people who ask him for things. He'll have us executed."

But then the wisest among them realizes: "Wait. He has no choice right now. The people love us. He's been advertising us for three months. The event is in three days. He can't kill us. He can't touch us. If there was ever a time to squeeze him for money, it's right now. After the event? Who knows what happens to us. But right now, we have power."

So they approach Fir'awn with confidence. فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ (When the magicians came) – they came with strength, with certainty, with boldness.

أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا (Will we definitely have a reward?) – they don't ask timidly. They assert: We're definitely getting paid, right? There's no doubt we have compensation coming?

But they don't stop there. They add leverage: إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (If we are the ones who overcome) – meaning, if we're going to win, we should definitely be paid. And if we're going to win but we're not getting paid, well, we might not be very motivated. Things might go wrong. We work harder when we're motivated. You want us to win, don't you?

They're squeezing him like a mafia organization demanding payment. And Fir'awn, who would normally execute people for such audacity, is trapped. He can't do anything. He needs them.

translation: He said, "Yes, and indeed, you will then be of those near [to me]
commentary:
قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

Fir'awn sees an opportunity in this situation. The magicians have expressed desire for money, but they haven't mentioned the other thing people crave when they become popular: power. And the rumors are already circulating that people might prefer an administration of magicians over continued loyalty to Fir'awn.

So Fir'awn does something shrewd. He doesn't just agree to pay them. He preempts their potential desire for power by offering it before they can ask.

قَالَ نَعَمْ (He said: Yes) – absolutely, I'll pay you well. No problem there.

وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (And indeed, you will be from those brought near) – you'll become cabinet members. You'll be made governors, ministers, inner circle advisors. I'm offering you these positions of power right now.

Why? Because if they have money and the people's love, the next thing they might think about is taking his throne. He's giving them the vice presidency so they never start thinking about the presidency. He's installing the software update before the virus of ambition can corrupt their minds.

Even in this moment of apparent generosity, Fir'awn reveals his political genius – and his desperation. He's bribing them with power because he knows his own position is precarious.

translation: Moses said to them, "Throw whatever you will throw
commentary:
قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ

The public event begins. The stage is set. The crowds have gathered. The magicians are prepared. And Musa (AS) addresses them with calm confidence:

قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ (Musa said to them) – not with fear, not with anxiety, but with the certainty of someone who knows the outcome before it happens.

أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ (Throw whatever you're going to throw) – go ahead. Show me what you've got. Do your thing. I'm ready.

There's no hesitation in his voice, no worry in his demeanor. He's giving them the floor, letting them go first, completely unconcerned about what they might do. This confidence itself is unnerving.

translation: So they threw their ropes and their staffs and said, "By the might of Pharaoh, indeed it is we who are predominant
commentary:
فَأَلْقَوْا۟ حِبَالَهُمْ وَعِصِيَّهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ

فَأَلْقَوْا۟ حِبَالَهُمْ وَعِصِيَّهُمْ (So they threw their ropes and their staffs) – their tools of illusion, carefully prepared, masterfully designed to create the impression of serpents moving, slithering, coming to life.

وَقَالُوا۟ بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ (And they said: By the might of Fir'awn) – this is the condition Fir'awn gave them. Remember, he promised them money and power, but he demanded something in return: restore my reputation. Make me look good. As you're winning, as you're defeating Musa (AS), you must say: We do this by the authority of the great Fir'awn. We win because Fir'awn is mighty.

Fir'awn's reputation is now entirely in the magicians' hands. They are his last hope for salvaging his public image.

إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (Indeed, we are surely the ones who will overcome) – they declare victory before it happens, confidently proclaiming their superiority. The crowd watches. The illusions are convincing. The magicians are skilled. This looks impressive.

translation: Then Moses threw his staff, and at once it devoured what they falsified
commentary:
فَأَلْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ

فَأَلْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ عَصَاهُ (Then Musa threw his staff) – one staff against hundreds of ropes and staffs. One action against their elaborate display.

فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ (and suddenly it was swallowing) – the word تَلْقَفُ (talqafu) is remarkable. It comes from لَقَفَ, which means to consume something quickly, in one motion, like a bird catching food mid-air with perfect coordination, or a crocodile taking prey in a single bite. Food that disappears into the mouth instantly, effortlessly.

It also describes when someone falls into a well and the earth just swallows them – gone in one moment. That's the kind of consumption being described here.

مَا يَأْفِكُونَ (what they were fabricating) – not "their ropes and staffs" but "their lies," their deceptions, their fabrications. Every single illusion they created, every piece of their magic, every deception they manufactured – all of it was consumed in an instant. Target practice. One after another, gone, gone, gone, gone. Hundreds of objects disappearing in seconds.

The crowd watches in stunned silence. What just happened? The magicians stand frozen, staring at what they just witnessed.

translation: So the magicians fell down in prostration [to Allah]
commentary:
فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سَـٰجِدِينَ

فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سَـٰجِدِينَ (So the magicians were thrown down in prostration) – the passive voice is crucial here. They didn't decide to prostrate. They didn't choose to fall. They were thrown. It's as though they couldn't even control their own bodies. The command of Allah ﷻ overtook them and they were thrown into sajdah.

Consider what this means. Musa (AS) threw his staff, and the magicians were thrown into prostration. One action from the messenger of Allah ﷻ causes an involuntary response from those who opposed him.

Now think about the crowd's perception. Before this moment, they believed: "One magic is just better than another magic. Their tricks versus his tricks, and his are more impressive." But the magicians know better. These are the elite, the سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ, the experts with decades of experience. They've studied every form of magic, every technique, every illusion. They know everything magic can do.

And when they see what just happened, they realize immediately: This is not magic. I know magic. I've been doing it for twenty years. I know its limits, its methods, its boundaries. What I just witnessed transcends all of that. This is something else entirely.

So they can't help themselves. They fall into prostration because they recognize they just witnessed a miracle from Allah ﷻ.

translation: They said, "We have believed in the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (They said: We believe in the Master of all the worlds) – these are the same people who, moments ago, were declaring: بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ (By the might of Fir'awn). Now they're declaring faith in the Master of all peoples, all nations, all of creation.

Fir'awn is confused. He's thinking: Is this part of your act? Is this how you're praising me? Because I told you to praise me. Is this some elaborate setup for round two?

They said "Master of all the worlds" – and Fir'awn considers himself to be that Master. So is this directed at me? Are you talking about me?

The ambiguity must be resolved immediately.

translation: The Lord of Moses and Aaron
commentary:
رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ

They clarify without hesitation: رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (The Master of Musa and Harun) – don't be confused, Fir'awn. We're not talking about you. We mean the Master of Musa (AS) and Harun (AS), the One who sent them, the One who just displayed His power through them.

This is a direct, public insult to Fir'awn. The very people who were supposed to protect and restore his reputation have now, in front of the largest crowd ever assembled in Egypt, destroyed it worse than ever before. The weapon he created to annihilate Musa (AS) has been turned against him, just like in the earlier conversation where his words were turned back against him.

The magicians have committed the ultimate betrayal – not just believing in Musa's message, but publicly declaring it at the moment they were supposed to be declaring Fir'awn's greatness.

translation: [Pharaoh] said, "You believed Moses before I gave you permission. Indeed, he is your leader who has taught you magic, but you are going to know. I will surely cut off your hands and your feet on opposite sides, and I will surely crucify you all
commentary:
قَالَ ءَامَنتُمْ لَهُۥ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱلسِّحْرَ فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ ۚ لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

Fir'awn scrambles for damage control. There's no time to huddle with the generals this time. He has to come up with something on the spot, in front of everyone.

قَالَ ءَامَنتُمْ لَهُۥ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ (He said: You believed in him before I gave you permission?) – as though their faith required his authorization, as though he still has control over their hearts.

إِنَّهُۥ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱلسِّحْرَ (Indeed, he is your chief, the one who taught you magic) – Fir'awn invents a conspiracy theory on the spot. This was all planned! Musa (AS) is your gang leader. He's the master magician who taught all of you. This whole thing was a setup!

But Fir'awn knows this is a lie even as he's saying it. He personally recruited these magicians from cities across Egypt. He knows Musa (AS) has been right there by the pyramids the whole time, not traveling around teaching magic. He knows the timeline doesn't work. But he's desperate. He needs to try something, anything, to salvage the situation.

Do the people believe this explanation? No. After three months of advertising these magicians as the nation's champions, after Fir'awn claiming to know everything that happens in Egypt, after his reputation for making people disappear for the slightest infraction – suddenly he didn't know about this massive conspiracy? It's absurd. No one is buying it.

But he continues: فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (Then you will surely know) – know what? What I'm capable of doing to traitors.

لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ (I will surely cut off your hands and your feet from opposite sides) – right hand and left foot, or left hand and right foot. Mutilation from opposite ends, creating maximum pain, maximum disability.

وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (And I will surely crucify you all) – not just execution, but public display. Your mutilated bodies will be nailed to tree trunks, hung up for everyone to see. You won't be killed quickly. You'll die slowly, bleeding out, suffering for hours or days, as a warning to anyone else who thinks about betraying me.

Why the public display? Why not execute them privately? Because this is meant to terrorize. People should see what happens to those who turn against Fir'awn. They should be so frightened of thinking like these magicians are thinking that they never dare consider it.

translation: They said, "No harm. Indeed, to our Lord we will return
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ لَا ضَيْرَ ۖ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ

The magicians respond with words that must have shaken Fir'awn to his core: قَالُوا۟ لَا ضَيْرَ (They said: No harm).

لَا ضَيْرَ – go ahead. Do your worst. We're not concerned. Your threats mean nothing to us. Cut us up. Crucify us. It doesn't matter.

Why? إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ (Indeed, we are returning to our real Master anyway) – we're going back to Him regardless. You can take our lives in this world, but you can't touch what happens after. You have no power over our ultimate destination.

These are people who, less than an hour ago, were working for Fir'awn, seeking his money and favor. Now they're telling him his worst threats are meaningless. That's the transformation that happens when someone witnesses the truth of Allah ﷻ with absolute certainty.

translation: Indeed, we aspire that our Lord will forgive us our sins because we were the first of the believers
commentary:
إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَـٰيَـٰنَآ أَن كُنَّآ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Then they turn to the audience, to the massive crowd gathered for this event, and they say: إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَـٰيَـٰنَآ (We only hope that our Master will forgive us our mistakes).

They acknowledge their past. They don't hide it. We made serious errors. We were the dominant force opposing the messenger of Allah ﷻ. We came here to destroy Musa (AS). We used our skills in service of falsehood. We have sins that need forgiveness.

أَن كُنَّآ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (Since we were the first of the believers) – and we hope He forgives us because we're accepting faith immediately, right here, right now, despite the consequences. We're the first among this nation to believe.

Between the lines, they're saying to the crowd: What are you waiting for? We were working for Fir'awn. We were supposed to destroy this message. We know magic better than anyone. And we're telling you: this is real. This is truth from Allah ﷻ. Don't make our mistake. Believe now.

This public testimony from the most unexpected sources – Fir'awn's own champions – must have planted seeds of doubt in countless hearts throughout that massive audience.

translation: And We inspired to Moses, "Travel by night with My servants; indeed, you will be pursued
commentary:
۞ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِىٓ إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses what happens next. While Fir'awn is busy dealing with the crisis of the magicians' conversion, while he's occupied with executing them and managing the fallout, Musa (AS) receives new instructions.

وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ (And We revealed to Musa) – direct communication from Allah ﷻ, clear guidance about what must happen next.

أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِىٓ (Take My slaves by night) – leave under cover of darkness. Don't wait. Don't delay. The window of opportunity is now, while Fir'awn is distracted.

إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ (You will surely be followed) – make no mistake about it. Once Fir'awn gets his house in order, once he finishes dealing with the magicians, he's coming after you next. You will be pursued. So move now, move quickly, and prepare for what's coming.

This is the wisdom of Allah ﷻ. The same event that was meant to destroy Musa (AS) – the public gathering, the magicians' challenge – instead created the perfect distraction for the escape of Bani Israel.

translation: Then Pharaoh sent among the cities gatherers
commentary:
فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ

Once Fir'awn discovers that Bani Israel have fled, he springs into action: فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (Then Fir'awn sent recruiters into all the cities).

This is a military draft. Recruiters go throughout the empire gathering forces. This isn't a small expedition. Fir'awn wants a massive army, every available soldier, every able-bodied man who can fight.

Why? Because he's not just going after Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) anymore. He wants genocide. He wants to wipe out the entire nation of Bani Israel. No survivors. No remnants. They've become too much of a threat. Better to eliminate them entirely and rebuild without them, even though they were free labor, even though they were essential to the economy.

He can't afford to lose his entire kingdom over these people. So they must be destroyed completely.

translation: [And said], "Indeed, those are but a small band
commentary:
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ

The recruiters go out with Fir'awn's propaganda message: إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ (Indeed, these are just a small band).

The word شِرْذِمَةٌ (shirdhimatun) refers to a tiny, powerless group. Think of old, worn, torn clothes – that's what شِرْذِمَةٌ suggests. They're insignificant. They're weak. They're hardly worth our attention, except that they pose a threat.

This is modern propaganda technique. When large populations are killed, media reports say "a few people died" to minimize the perceived injustice. Don't worry about the numbers. It's just a small group of powerless people. No big deal.

And notice: these are the same words used against the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the Muslims in Makkah. The parallel story is always present. The Quraysh also said: They're just a few. It's not a big deal if we eliminate them. It's not really genocide. It's pest control.

قَلِيلُونَ (few) – they're so small, so minimal, so unimportant. Let's just clean them out. Let's make our nation pure again.

translation: And indeed, they are enraging us
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُمْ لَنَا لَغَآئِظُونَ

وَإِنَّهُمْ لَنَا لَغَآئِظُونَ (And indeed, they have enraged us) – this carries two meanings, both of which serve Fir'awn's propaganda.

First meaning: These people have made us so angry. They've insulted us. They've caused problems. They've made our king look bad. They've turned our own magicians against us. We should be furious at them. Join the army and help us punish them for what they've done.

The recruiters go around inciting hatred. They give speeches about the threat these Muslims pose. They describe how Bani Israel will destroy Egypt if allowed to escape. They whip up nationalistic fervor: "These people are dangerous to our way of life. They must be stopped."

Sound familiar? This same technique is used throughout history, including today.

Second meaning: Those people are so enraged at us. They hate us. Look at how much they despise our great nation. We enslaved them, so they have deep hatred for us. If we let them go, they'll build an army somewhere and come back to attack us one day. We can't let them escape. We have to protect ourselves.

Both meanings work in Fir'awn's favor. Either way, the message is: these people must be eliminated for our safety and our honor.

translation: And indeed, we are a cautious society
commentary:
وَإِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ حَـٰذِرُونَ

وَإِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ حَـٰذِرُونَ (And indeed, we are all on guard) – the word حَـٰذِرُونَ means being watchful, alert, prepared for danger.

We're not attacking innocent people. We're defending ourselves. We're protecting our nation from a threat. We have to be vigilant. We have to take up shields. This is about our security, our survival.

The word حَـٰذِرُونَ describes a fear that's either actual or presumed. In this case, it's presumed. Bani Israel were enslaved for generations. They're fleeing with their families, their elderly, their children. They're not an invading army. But Fir'awn frames them as a presumed threat: If they get away, maybe one day they'll become dangerous. We can't take that chance.

This is how tyrants justify atrocities. It's not aggression – it's self-defense. It's not genocide – it's national security. We're just being careful. We're just protecting what's ours.

translation: So We removed them from gardens and springs
commentary:
فَأَخْرَجْنَـٰهُم مِّن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ

Now Allah ﷻ shifts perspective. The recruiters gave those speeches, made those arguments, rallied the troops. But Allah ﷻ says: فَأَخْرَجْنَـٰهُم (We brought them out) – not "they went out to fight" but We, Allah ﷻ, brought them out.

مِّن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (from gardens and springs) – these weren't just any cities. These were the most beautiful territories in the world at that time. Gardens along the Nile. Water springs throughout their lands. Breathtaking civilization, magnificent architecture, unparalleled wealth.

The Egyptians lived in luxury, in comfort, surrounded by beauty that other nations could only dream of. And when the call came to save the nation, to protect against this supposed threat, they left all of it behind.

We brought them out, Allah ﷻ says. This wasn't their idea. This was Our plan. We moved them out of their comfort, out of their gardens and springs, for what they thought was a quick military operation: We'll go handle this small group and come right back. Just some basic maintenance. We'll be home soon.

They never saw those gardens again.

translation: And treasures and honorable station
commentary:
وَكُنُوزٍ وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ

وَكُنُوزٍ (and treasures) – the wealth they accumulated, the gold, the resources, the economic prosperity that made Egypt the superpower of its era. They left all of it.

وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ (and a noble position) – they were the dominant civilization. They had status among all nations. When people thought of power, they thought of Egypt. When they thought of advancement, architecture, organization, strength – they thought of Egypt. That noble position, that international respect, that reputation – they left it all behind.

For what? To chase a small band of slaves into the desert. To eliminate a threat that didn't actually threaten them. To salvage the ego of one tyrant who couldn't accept that his authority had been challenged.

Allah ﷻ is saying: Look at what they abandoned. Look at what they gave up. All of that luxury, all of that power, all of that status – gone. And they'll never get it back.

translation: Thus. And We caused to inherit it the Children of Israel
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ

كَذَٰلِكَ (That is how it is) – this is how We operate. This is Our way.

وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (And We caused Bani Israel to inherit it) – eventually, We gave those gardens, those treasures, that land, that position to Bani Israel.

Not immediately, but eventually. There came a time when Bani Israel had an empire. Allah ﷻ gave them power that stretched even beyond what Sulaiman (AS) had. At one point, their influence covered almost the entire known world. The very people who were enslaved, who were considered worthless, whom Fir'awn tried to exterminate – Allah ﷻ elevated them to become inheritors of that same wealth, that same status, that same position of nobility.

This is mentioned in greater detail in Surah Al-Qasas. The tables turned completely. The oppressed became the elevated. The enslaved became the honored. And those who had everything lost it all.

translation: So they pursued them at sunrise
commentary:
فَأَتْبَعُوهُم مُّشْرِقِينَ

فَأَتْبَعُوهُم مُّشْرِقِينَ (So they pursued them at sunrise) – why is the time significant?

Because Musa (AS) and Bani Israel left in the middle of the night. They had a head start, moving under cover of darkness.
