If Fir'awn's army had left in the night as well, the cavalry and chariots would have caught up to them quickly. But they began pursuit as the sun rose, meaning Bani Israel were already far ahead.

However, there's a problem. Bani Israel includes men, women, children, elderly people, families, luggage, supplies. They're not a fast-moving military unit. They're a massive civilian population trying to escape. And behind them, a trained army with horses and chariots is gaining ground.

As they move, they end up at the water's edge. The sea is before them. The army approaches from behind. The earth begins to shake with the sound of thousands of soldiers marching. A cloud of dust rises in the distance. And 600,000 civilians realize they're trapped.

translation: And when the two companies saw one another, the companions of Moses said, "Indeed, we are to be overtaken
commentary:
فَلَمَّا تَرَٰٓءَا ٱلْجَمْعَانِ قَالَ أَصْحَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ

فَلَمَّا تَرَٰٓءَا ٱلْجَمْعَانِ (When the two groups saw each other) – the army spots Bani Israel. There they are! Hur

translation: [Moses] said, "No! Indeed, with me is my Lord; He will guide me
Then We inspired to Moses, "Strike with your staff the sea," and it parted, and each portion was like a great towering mountain
And We advanced thereto the pursuers
And We saved Moses and those with him, all together
Then We drowned the others
Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

This ayah concludes the story of Musa (AS) and Fir'awn with a powerful reminder: "In all of that, there is in fact a miraculous sign." The phrase "in all of that" refers not only to the dramatic events of the parting of the sea but to the entire narrative that preceded it. Allah ﷻ is establishing that these stories themselves constitute miracles—they should be sufficient evidence for those seeking truth.

The second part of the ayah addresses a recurring theme throughout the surah: "But most of them refuse to believe." This phrase appears multiple times, and each occurrence serves as a gentle reminder to the Prophet ﷺ, who was grieving over his people's rejection. Allah ﷻ is teaching him that even the most overwhelming signs do not guarantee belief, as faith ultimately requires a heart that is willing to accept truth.

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"And no doubt, your Master—He is in fact the ultimate authority, the always merciful." These two divine attributes—Al-Aziz (the All-Mighty) and Ar-Rahman (the Merciful)—appear together repeatedly in this surah. Despite the severe punishment that befell Fir'awn and his armies, Allah ﷻ remains both powerful and merciful. He has the authority to deliver punishment immediately, yet His mercy delays it, giving people opportunity after opportunity to turn back to Him.

translation: And recite to them the news of Abraham
commentary:
وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ

Allah ﷻ now commands the Prophet ﷺ: "And recite unto them the story of Ibrahim." The word نَبَأَ (naba) refers to news or information that is momentous and previously unknown. This transition is strategic—the Quraysh have just demanded physical miracles, claiming they won't believe without spectacular signs from the sky. Allah ﷻ responds by saying: those requests are denied, and here's why. Now let's examine your own religious foundations instead.

The shift to Ibrahim's (AS) story is particularly meaningful because Ibrahim (AS) is the forefather the Arabs took great pride in. By presenting his story, Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: before you criticize My message and demand miracles, let's discuss what you actually believe and why.

translation: When he said to his father and his people, "What do you worship
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَا تَعْبُدُونَ

"When he said to his father and his entire nation: What do you people worship?" Ibrahim (AS) begins with a simple, direct question. He doesn't start with accusations or condemnations—he asks them to explain their own beliefs. The word تَعْبُدُونَ (ta'budun) comes from the root meaning to enslave oneself, highlighting that worship is fundamentally about whom you choose to serve.

This question is being echoed by the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to his own people. Just as Ibrahim (AS) confronted his nation's idol worship, the Prophet ﷺ is now confronting the Quraysh with the same fundamental question about their beliefs.

translation: They said, "We worship idols and remain to them devoted
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ نَعْبُدُ أَصْنَامًا فَنَظَلُّ لَهَا عَـٰكِفِينَ

"They said: We worship idols, and we remain in devotion before them." The word عَـٰكِفِينَ (akifin) implies sitting in devoted meditation or spiritual retreat. They're describing an elaborate religious practice with its own rituals and traditions. Behind this simple response lies an entire mythology—stories about what each idol represents, which forces of nature they control, the history of how they came to be worshipped.

Their answer reveals they do have a religious system, one passed down through generations with its own internal logic and emotional attachments.

translation: He said, "Do they hear you when you supplicate
commentary:
قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ

Ibrahim (AS) responds with another question: "Do they hear you when you call?" He's not attacking yet—he's continuing to probe the logical foundations of their belief. If you're worshipping these idols, praying to them, sitting in devotion before them, can they actually hear your prayers?

This is the methodology of rational inquiry. Ibrahim (AS) is teaching us how to engage with false beliefs—not through emotional appeals or mockery at first, but through systematic questioning that reveals logical inconsistencies.

translation: Or do they benefit you, or do they harm
commentary:
أَوْ يَنفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ

"Or are they able to benefit you? Or to harm you?" These questions cut to the heart of why anyone would worship something. The purpose of worship, from a purely functional perspective, is to seek benefit and avoid harm. If these idols can do neither, what's the point?

Notice the progression: First, can they hear? Second, can they respond by benefiting or harming? Ibrahim (AS) is building a logical case, question by question.

translation: They said, "But we found our fathers doing thus
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ بَلْ وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا كَذَٰلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ

When confronted with questions they cannot answer rationally, they retreat to tradition: "We found our ancestors doing exactly this." This is one of the most common barriers to truth throughout history—the appeal to ancestral practice. "This is how we've always done things. This is what our parents did, and their parents before them."

This response reveals a critical failure: when you cannot defend your beliefs with reason, you defend them with tradition alone. This is how many Muslims today practice Islam—not because they understand it, but because "this is what my family does." Ibrahim (AS) is teaching us that true faith must be rooted in understanding, not blind tradition.

## Ayah 75-76

قَالَ أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ / أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُمُ ٱلْأَقْدَمُونَ

"Have you actually looked at what you've been worshiping—you and your most ancient ancestors?" The word أَفَرَءَيْتُم (afara'aytum) implies really seeing, truly observing with understanding. It's not just about physical sight but about comprehending reality.

Ibrahim (AS) is asking them to step back and actually examine their practice objectively. Don't just follow blindly—look at what you're doing. See it for what it really is. Your ancestors whom you respect so much—have you considered whether they truly saw the reality of what they worshipped?

translation: He said, "Then do you see what you have been worshipping
You and your ancient forefathers
Indeed, they are enemies to me, except the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِّىٓ إِلَّا رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"Then they are an enemy to me—except the Master of all nations." After systematically dismantling their arguments, Ibrahim (AS) makes his declaration. All these objects of worship, all these ancient ancestors who led people astray—they are enemies to him. The word عَدُوٌّ (enemy) is singular even though it refers to multiple idols and people, suggesting they function as one united force of opposition against truth.

The exception—"except the Master of all nations"—establishes that there is only One worthy of worship, One who is not an enemy but the true Master.

translation: Who created me, and He [it is who] guides me
commentary:
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَنِى فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ

"The One who created me, and He is the One who guides me." Ibrahim (AS) now begins describing his true Lord through His attributes and actions. Creation and guidance are linked—the One who created cannot have created without purpose, without direction. If He created me, He must guide me. This is a profound statement about the necessity of divine guidance.

translation: And it is He who feeds me and gives me drink
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِى هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِى وَيَسْقِينِ

"And He is the One who feeds me and gives me drink." Ibrahim (AS) continues with the most basic necessities of life—food and water. These daily provisions come from Allah ﷻ alone. While this may seem simple, it's a powerful reminder that our most fundamental needs are met by Him, not by idols or ancestors or our own power.

translation: And when I am ill, it is He who cures me
commentary:
وَإِذَا مَرِضْتُ فَهُوَ يَشْفِينِ

"And whenever I become sick, He is the One who cures me." Notice the beautiful shift in pronouns here. Everything else—creation, guidance, feeding, giving drink—is attributed directly to Allah ﷻ. But sickness? Ibrahim (AS) says "whenever I become sick" rather than "when He makes me sick." Out of profound respect and good manners with Allah ﷻ, he attributes the sickness to himself but the cure to Allah ﷻ.

This teaches us proper etiquette (adab) when speaking about Allah ﷻ—attribute all good to Him directly, but when mentioning difficulty, use neutral or self-referential language.

translation: And who will cause me to die and then bring me to life
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِى يُمِيتُنِى ثُمَّ يُحْيِينِ

"And the One who will give me death, then bring me back to life." Ibrahim (AS) now speaks of the ultimate transition—from life to death to resurrection. This is the only Being with power over life and death, the only One who can bring someone back after they have died.

translation: And who I aspire that He will forgive me my sin on the Day of Recompense
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لِى خَطِيٓـَٔتِى يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ

"And the One from whom I hope that He will forgive my mistakes on the Day of Judgment." The word أَطْمَعُ (atma'u) expresses deep hope and eager expectation. Despite being a prophet, Ibrahim (AS) speaks with complete humility about his need for forgiveness. He uses خَطِيٓـَٔتِى (khatiyati), indicating errors and mistakes—plural in meaning though singular in form.

This echoes what the magicians said after believing in Musa's (AS) message: "We hope our Master will forgive our mistakes." The righteous, no matter how elevated their station, always remain conscious of their need for Allah's forgiveness.

translation: [And he said], "My Lord, grant me authority and join me with the righteous
commentary:
رَبِّ هَبْ لِى حُكْمًا وَأَلْحِقْنِى بِٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

Here Ibrahim (AS) transitions from describing Allah ﷻ in the third person to addressing Him directly in prayer. This is the same beautiful transition found in Surah Al-Fatihah—when you remember Allah's ﷻ attributes and blessings, you realize He can hear you, so you turn to Him directly.

"My Master, grant me sound judgment and join me with the righteous." The word حُكْمًا (hukman) means wisdom, sound judgment, and firm understanding. This was the same request Musa (AS) made, and now we see it's a timeless prayer of all prophets—grant me firmness in faith and understanding.

"Join me with the righteous"—even though Ibrahim (AS) will become among the greatest of the righteous, he asks to be joined with them, showing humility and recognition that righteousness is a gift from Allah ﷻ.

translation: And grant me a reputation of honor among later generations
commentary:
وَٱجْعَل لِّى لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ

"And give me a truthful reputation among those who come after me." The phrase لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ (lisana sidq—tongue of truth) means a reputation, a legacy, a way of being remembered that is truthful and honorable.

Ibrahim (AS) was traveling extensively, encountering various false religions, and he was often alone in his message. He worried: who will remember what I taught? Who will carry this message forward? I'm not establishing a nation whose children will tell their story for generations. So he made this supplication—let my message, Your message through me, continue to be told truthfully to those who come after.

SubhanAllah, look at how Allah ﷻ answered this prayer. To this day, over 3,000 years later, we tell Ibrahim's (AS) story. Every Muslim knows his stand against idolatry, his submission to Allah ﷻ, his legacy. We are the fulfillment of this prayer.

translation: And place me among the inheritors of the Garden of Pleasure
commentary:
وَٱجْعَلْنِى مِن وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

"And make me among the inheritors of the Garden of perpetual bliss." We just learned about nations leaving behind gardens, and other nations inheriting them temporarily—but eventually they too would lose them. Ibrahim (AS) asks to inherit a garden that is marked by permanent blessing (نَّعِيمِ), one that never diminishes or disappears.

translation: And forgive my father. Indeed, he has been of those astray
commentary:
وَٱغْفِرْ لِأَبِىٓ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ

"And forgive my father—he has been among those who are lost." This is a deeply emotional request. Ibrahim (AS) acknowledges his father is from those in misguidance (ٱلضَّآلِّينَ), yet out of natural human compassion and family bonds, he asks Allah ﷻ to forgive him.

Later, Allah ﷻ would inform Ibrahim (AS) that he should stop asking forgiveness for his father, as he was an enemy of Allah ﷻ. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ experienced something similar with his uncle Abu Talib. This teaches us an important lesson: we have responsibilities toward our parents in matters of faith. We must continue trying to guide them, invite them to truth, but ultimately their choices are their own.

translation: And do not disgrace me on the Day they are [all] resurrected
commentary:
وَلَا تُخْزِنِى يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ

"And don't humiliate me on the Day they are all raised." Previously, Ibrahim (AS) mentioned his father specifically, but now he says "the Day when they are raised"—using the plural. He's acknowledging that his father has become part of the wrongdoing nation. He cannot separate him from them anymore.

Ibrahim (AS) is asking: don't let my father become a source of humiliation for me on Judgment Day because of these family ties. This is especially poignant because Allah ﷻ had already informed him to stop praying for his father. Now Ibrahim (AS) says: since You've told me not to pray for him anymore, at least don't let him become a source of my humiliation on that Day.

translation: The Day when there will not benefit [anyone] wealth or children
commentary:
يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ

"The Day when neither wealth nor children will bring benefit." Notice that Ibrahim (AS) was just talking about his father, yet he doesn't say "wealth and parents won't benefit"—he says "wealth and children." Why? Because his father is already a closed case. Allah ﷻ told him his father cannot benefit him, not even in this world. So the only family hope one might have is children. But even then, Ibrahim (AS) acknowledges that on Judgment Day, even children and wealth cannot benefit someone.

translation: But only one who comes to Allah with a sound heart
commentary:
إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى ٱللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ

"Except the one who comes to Allah with a sound heart." This is the exception—the only thing that benefits on that Day is the state of your heart. The word سَلِيمٍ (salim) means sound, healthy, whole, peaceful, secure.

But there's additional depth here: someone whose wealth and children DO benefit them on Judgment Day is someone who came with a sound heart. This means they spent their wealth correctly, raised their children properly. That wealth testified for them. Those children became a source of blessing for them. The condition is the sound heart—when you have that, even wealth and children become beneficial.

translation: And Paradise will be brought near [that Day] to the righteous
commentary:
وَأُزْلِفَتِ ٱلْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

"And Paradise will be brought close to those who had taqwa." The word أُزْلِفَتِ (uzlifat) means to bring something near, to make it accessible. They haven't entered yet, but Paradise is so close they can see it clearly. This is specifically for those who protected themselves through consciousness of Allah ﷻ (ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ).

translation: And Hellfire will be brought forth for the deviators
commentary:
وَبُرِّزَتِ ٱلْجَحِيمُ لِلْغَاوِينَ

"And the blazing Fire will be brought out face-to-face for those who went astray." The word بُرِّزَتِ (burrizat) means to bring something out into the open, to expose it, to present it directly before someone. The Hell-fire won't be hidden—it will be right in front of those who deviated from the path (ٱلْغَاوِينَ).

translation: And it will be said to them, "Where are those you used to worship
commentary:
وَقِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ

"And it will be said to them: Where is all that you used to worship?" Notice how these questions mirror exactly what Ibrahim (AS) asked his people. He asked: Do they hear you? Can they benefit or harm you? Now on Judgment Day, Allah ﷻ asks similar questions—but this time, those who worshipped false gods cannot respond with "we found our fathers doing it." Their fathers are standing right next to them in the same predicament.

translation: Other than Allah? Can they help you or help themselves
commentary:
مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ هَلْ يَنصُرُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَنتَصِرُونَ

"Besides Allah—will they help you now? Or can they even help themselves?" This is the ultimate test. You worshipped them claiming they had power. Now you're about to enter punishment—where are they? Can they rescue you? Can they even rescue themselves?

The questions expose the complete powerlessness of everything worshipped besides Allah ﷻ.

translation: So they will be overturned into Hellfire, they and the deviators
commentary:
فَكُبْكِبُوا۟ فِيهَا هُمْ وَٱلْغَاوُۥنَ

"Then they will be hurled into it headfirst—they and those who were astray." The word كُبْكِبُوا۟ (kubkibu) is an intensive form, suggesting being turned upside down and thrown forcefully. It comes from the root related to turning over pots and pans in the kitchen. The image is of being grabbed, flipped, and dropped face-first into the Fire.

"They"—referring to the idols themselves—"and those who were astray"—referring to those who worshipped them. Both the objects of false worship and their worshippers are hurled in together.

translation: And the soldiers of Iblees, all together
commentary:
وَجُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ أَجْمَعُونَ

"And the entire armies of Iblis, all of them together." This adds another dimension—the idols, the idol worshippers, and the devils who inspired this shirk in the first place. All are thrown in together.

This contrasts with the earlier story where Allah ﷻ saved the believers أَجْمَعُونَ (ajma'un—all of them together). There, everyone was saved. Here, everyone is destroyed.

translation: They will say while they dispute therein
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ وَهُمْ فِيهَا يَخْتَصِمُونَ

"They will say, while engaged in furious argument among themselves..." The word يَخْتَصِمُونَ (yakhtasimun) indicates intense, vicious debate and blame. They're fighting with each other, pointing fingers, trying to shift responsibility.

translation: By Allah, we were indeed in manifest error
commentary:
تَٱللَّهِ إِن كُنَّا لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

"By Allah! We were in such clear misguidance!" They swear by Allah ﷻ—desperately, trying to prove they recognize the truth now. "We were so confused, so clearly lost!" It's the swearing of people who have run out of options, who now see clearly but too late.

translation: When we equated you with the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
إِذْ نُسَوِّيكُم بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"When we made you equal to the Master of all the worlds." They're addressing their idols now, but they're really acknowledging the deeper reality. These idols were actually just products of Shaytan's armies. They weren't making stone equal to God—they were making the devils' deception equal to God.

The word نُسَوِّيكُم (nusawwikum—we made you equal) is devastating. They gave these powerless objects the same reverence, fear, hope, and devotion that belongs only to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And no one misguided us except the criminals
commentary:
وَمَآ أَضَلَّنَآ إِلَّا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ

"And no one misled us except the criminals." Now the blame-shifting begins. They look at the devils, the corrupt leaders, those who promoted this falsehood—"It was them! They're the criminals who led us astray!"

But this excuse falls flat because they had the same rational capacity Ibrahim (AS) used. They could have asked the same questions. They chose not to.

translation: So now we have no intercessors
commentary:
فَمَا لَنَا مِن شَـٰفِعِينَ

"So now we have no one to intercede for us." The word شَـٰفِعِينَ (shafi'in) refers to someone who intervenes on your behalf, who vouches for you, who tries to reduce your sentence. In that moment, they realize—no one is coming to speak for us. No one is coming to plead our case.

translation: And not a devoted friend
commentary:
وَلَا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ

"Nor any close, caring friend." The word حَمِيمٍ (hamim) refers to a friend who is intimate, deeply concerned about you, someone who genuinely cares. They have no intercessors and no true friends. All those social connections, all those relationships built on falsehood—gone.

translation: Then if we only had a return [to the world] and could be of the believers
commentary:
فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنَا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

"If only we had another chance, we would be among the true believers!" The word كَرَّةً (karrah) means a return, another shot, a do-over. This is the ultimate regret—"If only we could go back, we would believe this time!"

But this wish is futile. The time for belief has passed.

translation: Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

"In that there is certainly a sign, but most of them were not believers." The refrain returns. Allah ﷻ keeps reminding us—even with all these clear signs, even with Ibrahim's (AS) rational arguments, even with the warning of Judgment Day, most people still refuse to believe.

This is meant to console the Prophet ﷺ. Don't grieve excessively over their rejection. This has always been the pattern.

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"And indeed, your Master—He is the Mighty, the Merciful." Despite everything, Allah ﷻ remains both powerful enough to deliver immediate punishment and merciful enough to continue giving opportunities for repentance. These two names appear together throughout the surah, reminding us that divine justice is always balanced with divine mercy.

translation: The people of Noah denied the messengers
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"The people of Nuh lied against all the messengers." Although Nuh (AS) was the only messenger sent to them, rejecting him means rejecting the message that all messengers brought. To deny one is to deny all, because they all came with the same fundamental truth.

translation: When their brother Noah said to them, "Will you not fear Allah
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ نُوحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

"When their brother Nuh said to them: Will you not have taqwa?" The word "brother" emphasizes that Nuh (AS) was one of them—he spoke their language, understood their culture, lived among them. This made his message even more accessible, yet they still rejected it.

translation: Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger
commentary:
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ

"I am to you a trustworthy messenger." The word أَمِينٌ (amin) means trustworthy, honest, reliable—someone whose character is beyond reproach. Before claiming prophethood, these messengers had already established themselves as people of integrity in their communities.

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me." This is the dual call of every messenger: have taqwa of Allah ﷻ and follow the practical guidance I'm giving you. Some people say, "I believe in God, I don't need to follow a messenger." But this ayah shows the two are inseparable. Taqwa of Allah ﷻ manifests through obedience to His messengers.

Others say, "I'll follow the messenger but I don't really need to focus on God—just give me the rules." But the motivation for following the rules is taqwa. Without that consciousness of Allah ﷻ, obedience becomes hollow ritual.

translation: And I do not ask you for it any payment. My payment is only from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"And I don't ask you for any payment for this. My payment is only from the Master of all nations." This declaration appears with every prophet's story in this surah—establishing that true guidance never comes with a price tag. The prophets sought no worldly compensation, no power, no wealth. Their only reward was with Allah ﷻ.

This was particularly relevant to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, as the Quraysh accused him of seeking money and power through his message.

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me." The call is repeated, emphasizing its importance. True taqwa requires following the messenger's guidance.

translation: They said, "Should we believe you while you are followed by the lowest [class of people]
commentary:
۞ قَالُوٓا۟ أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَٱتَّبَعَكَ ٱلْأَرْذَلُونَ

"They said: Should we believe in you when the lowest of society follow you?" The word ٱلْأَرْذَلُونَ (al-ardhalun) refers to people considered undesirable, lower class, those without status or wealth. The elite of Nuh's (AS) nation couldn't bear the idea of being associated with "those people"—the poor, the powerless, the marginalized.

This objection appears in multiple prophet stories. The powerful don't want to humble themselves by joining a movement that includes the weak. They want exclusivity, privilege, special status.

translation: He said, "And what is my knowledge of what they used to do
commentary:
قَالَ وَمَا عِلْمِى بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ

"He said: And what knowledge do I have about what they used to do?" Nuh (AS) responds brilliantly—you're judging these people based on their past or their social status, but who am I to judge anyone? I don't have the right to evaluate people's worth the way you do.

translation: Their account is only upon my Lord, if you [could] perceive
commentary:
إِنْ حِسَابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّى ۖ لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ

"Their accounting is only with my Master—if only you could understand." Only Allah ﷻ knows what's truly in people's hearts. Only He can judge their real value and their deeds. If you truly understood this, you wouldn't be so quick to dismiss people based on worldly criteria.

translation: And I am not one to drive away the believers
commentary:
وَمَآ أَنَا۠ بِطَارِدِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

"And I am not going to drive away the believers." The word طَارِدِ (tarid) means to chase away, to expel, to treat as unwanted. Nuh (AS) firmly declares he will never push away sincere believers just because the elite consider them undesirable.

This was also an issue the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ faced. The leaders of Quraysh suggested: send away those poor followers of yours, and then we'll sit and listen to your message. The Quran firmly rejected this—never abandon the sincere believers for the sake of attracting the powerful.

translation: I am only a clear warner
commentary:
إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

"I am nothing but a clear warner." Nuh (AS) defines his role—he's not here to judge people, to create social hierarchies, or to build an elite following. He's simply here to deliver a clear warning about the consequences of rejecting truth.

translation: They said, "If you do not desist, O Noah, you will surely be of those who are stoned
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰنُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَرْجُومِينَ

"They said: If you don't stop this, O Nuh, you will certainly be among those stoned to death." After 950 years of patient preaching, this is their response—a death threat. The word ٱلْمَرْجُومِينَ (al-marjumin) refers to execution by stoning, a particularly brutal and public form of killing meant to humiliate and intimidate.

translation: He said, "My Lord, indeed my people have denied me
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِى كَذَّبُونِ

"He said: My Master, my people have denied me." Finally, after centuries of effort, Nuh (AS) makes his declaration to Allah ﷻ. Notice he says "my people"—there's still a sense of connection and pain in his words. They're his people, yet they've rejected him completely.

The word كَذَّبُونِ (kadhdhabuni) is definitive—they have thoroughly, completely rejected the message.

translation: Then judge between me and them with decisive judgement and save me and those with me of the believers
commentary:
فَٱفْتَحْ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحًا وَنَجِّنِى وَمَن مَّعِىَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

"So make an opening between me and them that is final, and save me and whoever is with me from among the believers." The word فَتْحًا (fathan) means an opening, but in this context it implies a decisive judgment, a clear separation. It also carries the meaning of conquest or victory.

Nuh (AS) is asking Allah ﷻ to make a definitive decision—separate us completely. Then he asks for salvation for himself and those who believed with him.

This moment should worry the Quraysh. When a prophet makes this supplication and Allah ﷻ responds, the rejecting nation is finished. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ had not yet made this final supplication against his people, so they still had time.

translation: So We saved him and those with him in the laden ship
commentary:
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ

"So We rescued him and those with him in the laden ship." The word ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (al-mashḥun) means fully loaded and moving forward. The ship was filled with believers and animals, and despite its heavy load, it moved forward smoothly—a miracle in itself.

translation: Then We drowned thereafter the remaining ones
commentary:
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا بَعْدُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ

"Then We drowned the rest after that." After the believers were safely aboard and the ship had departed, then the punishment came for everyone else. First salvation, then destruction—showing Allah's ﷻ mercy precedes His wrath.

translation: Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

"Indeed, in that is a sign, but most of them were not believers." The refrain returns. Even with such a dramatic story—950 years of preaching, the construction of a massive ship on dry land, the flood that covered everything—most people still didn't learn the lesson.

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"And indeed, your Master is the Mighty, the Merciful." Despite the severe punishment, Allah ﷻ is still described with these two attributes. He had the power to destroy them immediately but gave them 950 years of chances because of His mercy.

translation: Aad denied the messengers
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"The people of 'Ad denied the messengers." Another nation, another rejection. The story shifts now to people who were closer geographically to the Arabs—bringing the message even nearer to home.

translation: When their brother Hud said to them, "Will you not fear Allah
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

"When their brother Hud said to them: Will you not have taqwa?" The pattern repeats—the same opening, the same call. Every messenger speaks with one voice.

translation: Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger
commentary:
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ

"I am to you a trustworthy messenger."

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me."

translation: And I do not ask you for it any payment. My payment is only from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"And I don't ask you for any payment—my compensation is only with the Master of all nations." The same anthem continues, but now we'll see what specific evil characterized the people of 'Ad.

translation: Do you construct on every elevation a sign, amusing yourselves
commentary:
أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ ءَايَةً تَعْبَثُونَ

"Do you build monuments on every high place, just playing around?" The word رِيعٍ (ri'in) refers to elevated locations, landmarks, prominent places. The word ءَايَةً (ayah) here means a sign or monument—impressive structures built to show off.

The word تَعْبَثُونَ (ta'bathun) means to play, to do something without serious purpose, frivolously. They were using their wealth and advanced technology to build pointless monuments—architecture for the sake of showing off, not for any beneficial purpose.

This describes societies that become so wealthy they spend resources on decorative, frivolous construction while neglecting real needs. Think of modern cities with extravagant, purposeless monuments while basic infrastructure crumbles.

translation: And take for yourselves palaces and fortresses that you might abide eternally
commentary:
وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصَانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ

"And you build mighty structures hoping you'll live forever?" The word مَصَانِعَ (masani') refers to impressive, fortified buildings or elaborate construction projects. They built these massive structures as if they would never die, as if they would live forever.

This reveals their mindset—they had become so powerful and advanced that they forgot about death and accountability. Their architecture reflected their arrogance.

translation: And when you strike, you strike as tyrants
commentary:
وَإِذَا بَطَشْتُم بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ

"And when you strike, you strike as tyrants." The word بَطَشْتُم (batashtum) means to seize violently, to punish harshly. The word جَبَّارِينَ (jabbarin) refers to tyrants, those who show no mercy, who torture mercilessly.

Here's the contrast: externally, their civilization looked beautiful—impressive buildings, advanced architecture. But when it came to how they treated people, especially the weak, they were brutal tyrants. Their cities were civilized but their hearts were savage.

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me." The call returns after exposing their specific sins—frivolous wealth and tyrannical behavior.

translation: And fear He who provided you with that which you know
commentary:
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَمَدَّكُم بِمَا تَعْلَمُونَ

"And be conscious of the One who provided you with what you know well." Hud (AS) reminds them—all these resources, all this wealth, all this technology came from Allah ﷻ. You know this. You see His blessings all around you.

translation: Provided you with grazing livestock and children
commentary:
أَمَدَّكُم بِأَنْعَـٰمٍ وَبَنِينَ

"He provided you with livestock and children."

translation: And gardens and springs
commentary:
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ

"And gardens and springs." Look around you—these material blessings should make you grateful, should make you conscious of Him. Instead, you became arrogant.

translation: Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day
commentary:
إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

"I fear for you the punishment of a Great Day." Hud (AS) expresses genuine concern—not anger or judgment, but fear on their behalf. He sees where they're heading and he's trying to warn them.

translation: They said, "It is all the same to us whether you advise or are not of the advisors
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْوَٰعِظِينَ

"They said: It's all the same to us whether you preach to us or don't preach at all." This response is echoed throughout history—"Save your breath. We're not interested. Your advice doesn't affect us one way or another."

This was also said to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. No matter how sincere, how eloquent, how reasonable the message—some hearts are sealed.

translation: This is not but the custom of the former peoples
commentary:
إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا خُلُقُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

"This is nothing but the customs of the ancients." They dismiss the message as outdated mythology, old stories that don't apply to their advanced civilization. "This is just how ancient people thought. We've moved beyond this."

This is actually Allah's ﷻ comment about them—this attitude, this rejection, is nothing but the same behavior displayed by earlier generations.

translation: And we are not to be punished
commentary:
وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ

"And we're not going to be punished." They felt completely secure in their power. Look at our civilization! Look at our technology! Look at our mighty buildings! Who could possibly punish us?

This is the ultimate arrogance—believing you're too powerful, too advanced, too secure to face divine consequences.

translation: And they denied him, so We destroyed them. Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

"So they denied him, and We destroyed them. Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not believers." Their mighty buildings, their advanced civilization, their sense of security—none of it saved them. The word أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ (ahlaknāhum) indicates complete destruction, utter annihilation.

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"And indeed your Master is the Mighty, the Merciful." Despite their arrogance, despite their rejection, Allah ﷻ remains both powerful and merciful. These two attributes frame every story of punishment—showing that divine justice is never separated from divine mercy.

translation: Thamud denied the messengers
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"Thamud denied the messengers." Now we move to a nation even closer to the Arabs—the Thamud were their direct neighbors, and their ruins were visible landmarks the Quraysh passed regularly in their trade caravans.

translation: When their brother Salih said to them, "Will you not fear Allah
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ صَـٰلِحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

"When their brother Salih said to them: Will you not have taqwa?"

translation: Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger
commentary:
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ

"I am to you a trustworthy messenger."

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me."

translation: And I do not ask you for it any payment. My payment is only from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"And I don't ask you for any payment—my compensation is only with the Master of all nations." The same opening, the same truthful messenger, the same message. Now let's see what characterized Thamud's specific error.

## Ayah 146-147

أَتُتْرَكُونَ فِى مَا هَـٰهُنَآ ءَامِنِينَ / فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ

"Do you think you'll be left here secure—in gardens and springs?"

translation: Will you be left in what is here, secure [from death]
Within gardens and springs
And fields of crops and palm trees with softened fruit
commentary:
وَزُرُوعٍ وَنَخْلٍ طَلْعُهَا هَضِيمٌ

"And crops and date palms with tender fruit?" They enjoyed abundant natural resources—water, gardens, productive farmland. They felt secure in their prosperity.

translation: And you carve out of the mountains, homes, with skill
commentary:
وَتَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا فَـٰرِهِينَ

"And you carve homes from mountains, showing off your skill." The word تَنْحِتُونَ (tanhitun) means to carve, to sculpt. The word فَـٰرِهِينَ (farihin) means skillfully, but also with a sense of pride and showing off—doing it easily, without effort, to demonstrate superiority.

Like the people of 'Ad, they had technological advancement. But they used it frivolously, to show off rather than for genuine benefit.

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me."

translation: And do not obey the order of the transgressors
commentary:
وَلَا تُطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ

"And don't obey the command of those who go to excess." The word ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (al-musrifin) refers to those who exceed all bounds, who are extravagant and wasteful. Salih (AS) warns them against following the elite who live in excess and lead society toward corruption.

This is a warning against celebrity culture, against being obsessed with those who spend frivolously and live in luxury. Don't make them your role models. Don't follow their way.

translation: Who cause corruption in the land and do not amend
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ

"Those who cause corruption in the land and don't improve anything." These celebrities, these people of excess—they may donate to charity occasionally, they may do some good. But the corruption they cause vastly outweighs any good they do.

translation: They said, "You are only of those affected by magic
commentary:
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ

"They said: You are just someone who has been bewitched." The word ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ (al-musahharin) suggests someone who has had extensive magic performed on them, someone who is under a spell. "You're not thinking straight. You've been enchanted. This obsession of yours with religion—it's not natural."

translation: You are but a man like ourselves, so bring a sign, if you should be of the truthful
commentary:
مَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا فَأْتِ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ

"You're nothing but a human like us. So bring us a miracle if you're really truthful." Here it is again—the demand for a miracle. This is how the surah began—with the Quraysh demanding a miracle. Now we see nation after nation making the same demand.

translation: He said, "This is a she-camel. For her is a [time of] drink, and for you is a [time of] drink, [each] on a known day
commentary:
قَالَ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةٌ لَّهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ

"He said: This is a she-camel—she has her turn to drink, and you have your turn on a designated day." Allah ﷻ responded to their demand. He sent them a miraculous she-camel that would drink the entire water source in one day, then they could use it the next day.

The camel itself was miraculous—that one animal could consume so much water, and that this system would function as a test of their obedience.

translation: And do not touch her with harm, lest you be seized by the punishment of a terrible day
commentary:
وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

"And don't touch her with harm, or the punishment of a Great Day will seize you." The warning was clear—this is not just any camel. Don't harm her. Don't interfere with her. If you do, severe punishment will come.

translation: But they hamstrung her and so became regretful
commentary:
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ نَـٰدِمِينَ

"But they hamstrung her, and by morning they were regretful." The word عَقَرُوهَا (aqaruha) means to hamstring, to slash the tendons—the brutal way large animals were slaughtered. They killed the miraculous sign they had demanded.

The word نَـٰدِمِينَ (nadimin) means full of regret, remorseful. By morning, they realized what they had done. But regret after committing the sin doesn't erase the consequences.

translation: And the punishment seized them. Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

"So the punishment seized them. Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not believers." This story was especially relevant to the Quraysh—they passed by the ruins of Thamud regularly. They could see with their own eyes the remnants of this destroyed civilization. Yet even that visible evidence didn't make most of them believe.

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"And indeed your Master is the Mighty, the Merciful."

translation: The people of Lot denied the messengers
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"The people of Lut denied the messengers." Now we shift to a different type of societal corruption—sexual immorality at its most extreme.

translation: When their brother Lot said to them, "Will you not fear Allah
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

"When their brother Lut said to them: Will you not have taqwa?"

translation: Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger
commentary:
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ

"I am to you a trustworthy messenger."

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me."

translation: And I do not ask you for it any payment. My payment is only from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"And I don't ask you for any payment—my compensation is only with the Master of all nations."

translation: Do you approach males among the worlds
commentary:
أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلذُّكْرَانَ مِنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"Do you approach males from among all the worlds?" The word ٱلذُّكْرَانَ (al-dhukran) specifically refers to males. Lut (AS) is pointing out the absolute unnaturalness of their practice—of all the creation in all the worlds, you choose this?

One interpretation of مِنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ is: are your own women not attractive to you, so you've turned to males from other nations? Another interpretation: among all of creation, among all possibilities, this is what you choose?

translation: And leave what your Lord has created for you as mates? But you are a people transgressing
commentary:
وَتَذَرُونَ مَا خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ عَادُونَ

"And you abandon what your Master created for you from your spouses? Rather, you are a people who have transgressed all bounds." The word عَادُونَ (adun) means those who commit aggression, who cross boundaries, who declare enmity—in this case, enmity against Allah's ﷻ natural order.

This is the Quran's clear stance on homosexuality. It is not considered natural or genetic—it is considered a choice, and a profoundly wrong choice. This is not about hatred toward individuals but about clarity regarding the moral law. When fassha (sexual immorality) spreads in a society, when people become desensitized to boundaries, Shaytan offers them more and more deviant paths. The kinds of sexual crimes happening in the world today are unprecedented in history, and they're directly connected to the normalization of immorality.

This is a serious issue affecting both outside and inside the Muslim community. We need counselors, we need to address pornography addiction, we need to help our youth who may be struggling with these feelings. But we cannot compromise on the moral clarity of what Islam teaches.

translation: They said, "If you do not desist, O Lot, you will surely be of those evicted
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰلُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْرَجِينَ

"They said: If you don't stop, O Lut, you will certainly be among those expelled." They couldn't tolerate being questioned about their lifestyle. "This is a permissive society. Don't be a bigot. Tolerate us. And if you can't, then you don't belong here."

This response echoes today—anyone who questions or criticizes these behaviors is labeled intolerant and threatened with social expulsion.

translation: He said, "Indeed, I am, toward your deed, of those who detest [it]
commentary:
قَالَ إِنِّى لِعَمَلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَالِينَ

"He said: Indeed, I am toward your deed among those who detest it." Lut (AS) clarifies—he's not abandoning them as people, but he completely rejects and detests their actions. There's a difference between hating a behavior and hating a person.

translation: My Lord, save me and my family from [the consequence of] what they do
commentary:
رَبِّ نَجِّنِى وَأَهْلِى مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ

"My Master, save me and my family from what they do." Lut (AS) turns to Allah ﷻ in prayer—protect us from this society's corruption.

translation: So We saved him and his family, all
commentary:
فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ

"So We saved him and his family, all of them."

translation: Except an old woman among those who remained behind
commentary:
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ

"Except an old woman among those who remained behind." The word عَجُوزًا (ajuzan) specifically means an elderly woman—Lut's (AS) wife. The word ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (al-ghabirin) means those who got left behind, who couldn't keep up, who lost their way.

She was stubborn in her ways, attached to that society despite knowing the truth. So she was left behind, not saved with the believers.

translation: Then We destroyed the others
commentary:
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ

"Then We annihilated the others." The word دَمَّرْنَا (dammarna) indicates total destruction, complete devastation.

translation: And We rained upon them a rain [of stones], and evil was the rain of those who were warned
commentary:
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ

"And We rained upon them a rain—how evil was the rain of those who were warned!" Not a rain of water but a rain of stones, a meteor shower of destruction. They had been warned, yet they persisted, so the punishment came.

translation: Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

"Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not believers."

translation: And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

"And indeed your Master is the Mighty, the Merciful."

translation: The companions of the thicket denied the messengers
commentary:
كَذَّبَ أَصْحَـٰبُ لْـَٔيْكَةِ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ

"The people of the thicket denied the messengers." A new nation, but the pattern continues. The word ٱلْأَيْكَةِ (al-aykah) refers to a thicket or dense grove of trees—this identifies them by their location.

translation: When Shu'ayb said to them, "Will you not fear Allah
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ شُعَيْبٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ

"When Shu'ayb said to them: Will you not have taqwa?"

translation: Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger
commentary:
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ

"I am to you a trustworthy messenger."

translation: So fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"So be conscious of Allah and obey me."

translation: And I do not ask you for it any payment. My payment is only from the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

"And I don't ask you for any payment—my compensation is only with the Master of all nations."

translation: Give full measure and do not be of those who cause loss
commentary:
۞ أَوْفُوا۟ ٱلْكَيْلَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْسِرِينَ

"Give full measure and don't be among those who cause loss." Now we see what specific corruption characterized this nation—financial irregularities, fraudulent business practices, the ancient version of corporate corruption.

The word أَوْفُوا۟ (awfu) means to fulfill completely, to give exactly what is due. The word ٱلْمُخْسِرِينَ (al-mukhsirin) means those who create loss—not just shortchanging customers, but engaging in business practices that bankrupt others while meeting your own bottom line.

translation: And weigh with an even balance
commentary:
وَزِنُوا۟ بِٱلْقِسْطَاسِ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ

"And weigh with a scale that stands straight." The word ٱلْقِسْطَاسِ (al-qistas) refers to a balance scale, and ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمِ (al-mustaqim) means standing upright, balanced, fair.

Be absolutely fair in your measurements and transactions. Don't tilt the scale in your favor.

translation: And do not deprive people of their due and do not commit abuse on earth, spreading corruption
commentary:
وَلَا تَبْخَسُوا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ أَشْيَآءَهُمْ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ

"And don't shortchange people in their things, and don't go around the earth causing corruption." When you give people less than they deserve, when you cheat in business, you're not just harming individuals—you're creating a norm. Others see it and think, "If they're doing it, I can too."

One person crossing the line opens the door for others. Corruption spreads. That's why it's not just being corrupt—it's creating corruption, being مُفْسِدِينَ (mufsadin—those who cause corruption).

This is why the Quran addresses corruption at the individual level. Yes, you need regulatory institutions, laws, oversight. But until individuals take personal responsibility, until every shopkeeper, every business owner, every professional makes their scale stand straight, corruption won't end. Human greed is an individual problem that requires an individual solution—taqwa.

translation: And fear He who created you and the former creation
commentary:
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ وَٱلْجِبِلَّةَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

"And be conscious of the One who created you and the earliest generations." Be conscious of Allah ﷻ who created not just you but all of humanity before you. You're part of a vast creation, and He sees all of it.

translation: They said, "You are only of those affected by magic
commentary: قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُس

translation: You are but a man like ourselves, and indeed, we think you are among the liars
So cause to fall upon us fragments of the sky, if you should be of the truthful
He said, "My Lord is most knowing of what you do
And they denied him, so the punishment of the day of the black cloud seized them. Indeed, it was the punishment of a terrible day
Indeed in that is a sign, but most of them were not to be believers
And indeed, your Lord - He is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
And indeed, the Qur'an is the revelation of the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَنزِيلُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

After presenting numerous historical accounts of prophets and their nations, the Surah returns to its central theme: the Quran itself. Throughout these stories, there was an undercurrent of skepticism from the Quraysh, suggesting that the Quran wasn't enough evidence. They kept asking for more signs, more miracles. Allah ﷻ responds by saying: wasn't there a sign for them in this story? And in that account? And in this lesson? Each narrative carried its own evidence, its own proof.

Now, at the conclusion of these accounts, Allah ﷻ reintroduces the Quraysh to the Quran, as if saying: perhaps you haven't fully grasped what this Book truly is. The ayah states with absolute certainty: وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَنزِيلُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "No doubt, it is in fact the revelation sent down by the Master of all nations."

translation: The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down
commentary:
نَزَلَ بِهِ ٱلرُّوحُ ٱلْأَمِينُ

Throughout the Surah, every messenger declared: "I am an honest messenger." This theme of honesty and trustworthiness echoes repeatedly. Now Allah ﷻ reveals that the Quran came down through the hands of the ultimately honest and trustworthy being: الرُّوحُ ٱلْأَمِينُ - the Trustworthy Spirit, referring to the angel Jibreel (AS).

The messenger who delivers this revelation to an honest messenger is himself honest. There are layers of trustworthiness here: the message originates from the Most Trustworthy, Allah ﷻ, delivered by the trustworthy Jibreel (AS), to the trustworthy Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

translation: Upon your heart, [O Muhammad] - that you may be of the warners
commentary:
عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ

The revelation descended عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ - upon your heart, O Muhammad ﷺ. This wasn't merely information transmitted to the mind, but guidance embedded deep within the heart. The purpose is clear: لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ - so that you can become from those who warn.

This connects powerfully to what came before. Every single prophet mentioned in this Surah shared this role - none of them primarily gave good news in these accounts. None spoke extensively about Paradise or its rewards. Rather, they all came with clear warnings. They all said: "I've come to warn you clearly, and I'm not asking you for money."

At the end of all these stories, the Prophet ﷺ is told that the Quran has been given to him, and one of its fundamental functions is precisely this: to enable him to become from those who warn.

translation: In a clear Arabic language
commentary:
بِلِسَانٍ عَرَبِىٍّ مُّبِينٍ

The Quran came بِلِسَانٍ عَرَبِىٍّ مُّبِينٍ - in clear, clarifying Arabic speech. This quality of the revelation deserves deep reflection. You won't truly taste the warning of the Quran, won't genuinely experience its power, unless you engage with it in Arabic. Whatever level you can reach, whatever you can learn of this language, you must be motivated to pursue it. Once you do, its warnings will strike you like never before when you read them in the original, when you listen to them as they were revealed.

The word مُّبِينٍ - clear and clarifying - was used earlier in the Surah for the snake that Musa (AS) produced. When people saw that python emerge from the staff, there was no doubt left, no ambiguity. It clarified everything Musa (AS) had been saying. That's what مُّبِينٍ means: possessing that quality of absolute clarity.

The Quran is called mubin, and Arabic is called mubin. The Quran says إِنَّا أَنزَلْنَاهُ قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا - "We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran" elsewhere, and here it emphasizes عَرَبِىٍّ مُّبِينٍ. What's the significance of this pairing? The clarity of the Quran rests upon the clarity of the Arabic language. They're inextricably tied together.

Most controversies in Islamic studies, most ayahs that are misinterpreted or twisted, most attempts to reinvent meanings of certain passages - after years of study, I've discovered that the most comprehensive responses to these controversial interpretations don't lie in anything the way they lie in the language itself. When you genuinely conduct thorough Arabic language research into an ayah, controversies dissolve, misinterpretations vanish. But it requires deep engagement, not superficial study - clear Arabic, thorough Arabic.

Being true to the Book means being true to its language. This point is being emphasized here in this Surah because Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: Look, you have the best possible teacher in the Prophet ﷺ, the best possible curriculum in this Quran, and it's being delivered in your own language - a language you can understand with perfect clarity. This should make it abundantly clear to you that these words are not his. The Arabic of the Quran is so exceptional that the Prophet ﷺ himself wouldn't be capable of producing it, nor would any human being for that matter. This should be evident from the Arabic alone.

When the Quran is recited properly by someone who understands what they're doing, it's mesmerizing - especially with the shorter surahs. You're taken on a journey. Even though translation is important and necessary, I cannot maintain that rhythm and power in explanation. My recommendation: once you listen to and understand this Surah, you must listen to it being recited. That recitation is the necessary completion of the experience. It's essential because that's part of the original divine experience that's supposed to be there. The recitation carries effects that explanation alone cannot capture.

translation: And indeed, it is [mentioned] in the scriptures of former peoples
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِى زُبُرِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

Allah ﷻ declares: وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِى زُبُرِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ - and no doubt it is found in the earliest scrolls, in the scriptures of the ancients. Large portions of earlier revelations contained mention of this Quran, prophecies of this final revelation.

translation: And has it not been a sign to them that it is recognized by the scholars of the Children of Israel
commentary:
أَوَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ ءَايَةً أَن يَعْلَمَهُۥ عُلَمَـٰٓؤُا۟ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ

The mention of the Quran in previous scriptures has always been there, foretelling that the final revelation would come. So you're asking for a miracle? You want proof? Isn't it enough evidence for them that the scholars of Bani Israel have already recognized that the final Book was coming?

The Quraysh knew this. When they used to go into battle with the Jews of Medina, the Jews would warn them: "The final revelation is coming. The final messenger is coming. And when he arrives, he will wipe you out like 'Ad was wiped out, like Thamud was destroyed, like the people of Nuh (AS) were annihilated." The Israelites used to tell them this explicitly when they fought with them.

So Allah ﷻ is saying: isn't that enough of a sign for them? They're looking for miraculous evidence, yet the scholars who study the previous scriptures already know about this Quran.

## The Language Barrier They Create

### Ayahs 198-199

وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضِ ٱلْأَعْجَمِينَ • فَقَرَأَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ presents a powerful hypothetical: had We revealed this Quran upon some foreigner, some non-Arab, and then he read it to them, they wouldn't have believed in it. They would have immediately dismissed it, saying: "It's not in our language! Give it to us in our language!"

The irony is devastating. Aren't you grateful that it's in your own language? You don't have to make any effort to learn it - it's already your mother tongue. Yet you reject it.

translation: And even if We had revealed it to one among the foreigners
And he had recited it to them [perfectly], they would [still] not have been believers in it
Thus have We inserted disbelief into the hearts of the criminals
commentary:
كَذَٰلِكَ سَلَكْنَـٰهُ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ

كَذَٰلِكَ سَلَكْنَـٰهُ - that is how We have penetrated it deep inside the hearts of the criminals. The word سَلَكْنَـٰهُ comes from inserting something deep, sticking it deep inside.

There's a psychological phenomenon called cognitive dissonance: when someone hears words but processes them as though they're speaking a foreign language. They block everything out, understanding nothing, refusing to process what's being said. Allah ﷻ explains that this mentality, this complete blockage, has been embedded deep inside their hearts. They're acting toward the Quran as though it's in a foreign language they can't comprehend, even though it's in their own Arabic.

Earlier, Allah ﷻ said that most of them won't believe. Now He's explaining why: they've deserved to lose their ability to pay attention to this Quran. It's a consequence of their choices, their rejection.

translation: They will not believe in it until they see the painful punishment
commentary:
لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ

They're not going to believe in it until they see the painful punishment. Just like all the previous nations mentioned in this Surah, that's how it's going to be for this group as well.

### Ayahs 202-203

فَيَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ • فَيَقُولُوا۟ هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنظَرُونَ

That punishment will come to them بَغْتَةً - suddenly, out of nowhere, without any warning. They'll have no realization, no comprehension of what just happened. Then, in that moment of shock, they'll ask: هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنظَرُونَ - "Are we going to be given more time? Can we get another chance? Can you just hold off on this punishment? Will we be given a second opportunity?"

## The Irony of Their Rush to Punishment

translation: And it will come to them suddenly while they perceive [it] not
And they will say, "May we be reprieved
So for Our punishment are they impatient
commentary:
أَفَبِعَذَابِنَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ flips the timeline. There are two different moments being discussed here. In one moment - on Judgment Day - punishment comes and these people desperately beg: "Can we get just a little more time? Please, just a second chance?"

But right now, in this present moment, Allah ﷻ asks: أَفَبِعَذَابِنَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ - "Is it Our punishment that they're asking to be hastened?" They're saying: "Why don't you bring this punishment? Why are you waiting? You keep talking about the sun and moon crashing, people rising from graves - let's see it! Bring it on!"

Look at the devastating irony: right now they're rushing toward Allah's punishment, mocking it, demanding it. But when it actually comes, they'll be the first ones begging for it to stop, pleading for more time.

### Ayahs 205-207

أَفَرَءَيْتَ إِن مَّتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ سِنِينَ • ثُمَّ جَآءَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ • مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُمَتَّعُونَ

Allah ﷻ asks the Prophet ﷺ to observe, to consider: what if We provided for them, gave them wealth and sustenance, for a number of years? Then, just as punishment was about to strike, We stopped it right before impact and granted their request: "Okay, you want more time? Here are a few more years. Then We'll bring it down on you."

Even if that happened - even if punishment was delayed and they were given extra years - مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم - it wouldn't benefit them at all. Whatever they enjoyed during that time wouldn't come to their aid in any way. Once the heart is deviated to this degree, extra time makes no difference.

Think of a student who fails an exam and begs the teacher: "Please, just give me one more chance! Just one makeup exam, that's all I ask!" The teacher agrees: "Fine, next Friday." Does that student go home and immediately start studying? No. He thinks: "Next week? I've got time." He plays video games, watches movies, hangs out with friends. The whole week passes. When Friday arrives and he shows up for the makeup exam, he says: "Something came up this week. It was really difficult. I had a family situation." What he means by "family situation" is that his mom was upset because he spent the whole week watching movies, but he won't spell that out.

When people are procrastinators, when their hearts are set on rejection, no amount of extra time makes a difference.

## Allah's Justice in Destruction

### Ayahs 208-209

وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا لَهَا مُنذِرُونَ • ذِكْرَىٰ وَمَا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ

We never destroy a nation except when it has warners. The logic is clear: if there's a warner, there are only two possible outcomes - either you take the warning and survive, or you don't take the warning and face destruction. Those are the only two options.

ذِكْرَىٰ - that's a reminder. Usually Allah ﷻ says ذَٰلِكَ (that), but here He says ذِكْرَىٰ by itself, like an exclamation mark. That was a reminder! I'm just letting you know, reminding you of a powerful truth: the messenger, whenever he comes, represents the fact that We don't destroy unless there's been warning. And now that there is a warner among you, you're qualified for destruction.

وَمَا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ - and We are never, ever ones who do wrong. When people argue about why Allah ﷻ destroys towns and nations, this is the answer: We've never been wrongdoers. We give enough warning. We provide every opportunity. Destruction comes only after clear warning has been delivered and rejected.

## The Source of Revelation

### Ayahs 210-212

وَمَا تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ • وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُمْ وَمَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ • إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ

The devils don't bring down this Quran. They don't come with it. وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُمْ - it's not becoming of them anyway, not appropriate for them. They don't have that kind of capacity. وَمَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ - and they can't do it even if they wanted to.

إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ - they are completely denied access to hear it. مَعْزُولُونَ means forbidden, blocked, denied. When stars are shot at them to prevent them from eavesdropping on the heavens, they're forbidden from hearing. They have no access to this revelation whatsoever.

We learn from other parts of the Quran that angels and jinn exist close to each other in terms of interaction. One is a creature of light, the other of fire. They do sometimes have interaction, and jinn occasionally steal information from angels. But when this Quran came, when Jibreel (AS) - who is described elsewhere as الْقُرْآنِ الْكَرِيمِ (the Noble Quran) and in other places as the one who guards the trust - descended with it, he came with an entire battalion of angels surrounding the revelation. The jinn couldn't even get close enough to steal a tiny portion of it. They have zero access to see it or hear it.

## Commands to the Prophet

translation: Then have you considered if We gave them enjoyment for years
And then there came to them that which they were promised
They would not be availed by the enjoyment with which they were provided
And We did not destroy any city except that it had warners
As a reminder; and never have We been unjust
And the devils have not brought the revelation down
It is not allowable for them, nor would they be able
Indeed they, from [its] hearing, are removed
So do not invoke with Allah another deity and [thus] be among the punished
commentary:
فَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُعَذَّبِينَ

After establishing the divine origin and protection of the Quran, Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ directly: فَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ - don't you dare call upon anyone besides Allah ﷻ to worship. If you were to do so, then you would become from those who get tortured.

This isn't a suggestion that the Prophet ﷺ was at risk of shirk. Rather, it emphasizes the absolute severity of associating partners with Allah ﷻ. If even the Prophet ﷺ were hypothetically warned about this, how much more should everyone else take this warning seriously?

translation: And warn, [O Muhammad], your closest kindred
commentary:
وَأَنذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ ٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ

The Surah has dedicated so much space to telling the Prophet ﷺ: most of them won't believe, most of them won't believe, most of them won't believe. This message is repeated throughout. Now Allah ﷻ says: Okay, most won't believe. So start with your family.

وَأَنذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ ٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ - warn your close family members, your extended family. عَشِيرَة also means the men of your family, the responsible ones, the influential members. Find those closest to you and warn them. You can't reach the whole nation right now? Just start with the closest members of your family.

translation: And lower your wing to those who follow you of the believers
commentary:
وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Then comes a beautiful instruction about how to treat the believers who do follow him: وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ - lower your wing, humble yourself before them. This imagery of lowering the wings - where have we seen it before? Children are commanded to lower their wings before their parents as a sign of respect and humility.

Now Allah ﷻ tells the Messenger ﷺ that he should lower his wings to the Sahaba (RA). He should be genuinely humble in dealing with the companions, extra courteous, extraordinarily kind, going out of his way to be generous with them. Why? Because they are a tremendous blessing, a huge favor.

Just as parents are a blessing to children, the Sahaba (RA) are being described as a blessing to the Prophet ﷺ. Take extra care of them. The few who have believed are so valuable, so precious - treat them like treasures.

Appreciate this reciprocal relationship: on one hand, you have the followers of the Prophet ﷺ who love him beyond measure, following him no matter what the cost. They closely follow him. And because they so closely follow him, he is commanded to be extraordinarily kind and humble toward them. When he shows them that kindness and humility, it makes them even more devoted, even more loyal followers.

There's a profound truth here: people become devoted to goodness. When a leader is genuinely good to people, they respond with loyalty. When the leader shows humility, they become even more committed. So the Prophet ﷺ is told: go out of your way and show humility to those who have followed you.

Notice the distinction: you don't have to be humble to the leaders of Quraysh who reject you. You don't have to do that. But to the believers who follow you, show complete humility.

So there are two groups that the Prophet's attention should focus on: one, continue to warn your close family members, the closest of them. Two, be extraordinarily grateful for and humble toward your followers.

translation: And if they disobey you, then say, "Indeed, I am disassociated from what you are doing
commentary:
فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ فَقُلْ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

But then comes a crucial caveat: فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ - if they disobey you, whether family or followers, if they disobey you, then tell them: إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - "I have nothing to do with what you're doing. I'm completely disassociated from your actions."

This ayah calls for serious reflection. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't say "if they disbelieve in you" - He says "if they disobey you." At this point in Makkah, what does it mean to disobey the Prophet ﷺ? Hijab hasn't been revealed yet. Food restrictions haven't been revealed. The prohibition of alcohol hasn't come down. The five daily prayers haven't been established. Hajj obligations haven't been revealed. Fasting hasn't been prescribed. None of what we typically think of as Islamic behavioral requirements has been revealed yet.

So what exactly are they obeying or disobeying? The only work right now is the work of being with the Prophet ﷺ in his mission of delivering Islam to people. That's the sole mission at this point. All the other detailed instructions of Islam would predominantly come later in Medina.

So how can they disobey? What disobedience could they show? We understand here that being fully engaged with the mission of the Prophet ﷺ, the mission itself, is an act of obedience. And abandoning that mission is an act of disobedience.

They might say: "We have other things to do. We're busy. We don't have time for this mission." That's considered disobedience. The commitment to the prophetic mission is itself the primary obligation at this stage.

## Relying on Allah Alone

translation: And rely upon the Exalted in Might, the Merciful
commentary:
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْرَّحِيمِ

Just as the Prophet ﷺ was instructed at the beginning of the Surah, he's reminded again at the end: وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْرَّحِيمِ - continue to rely on the Ultimately Powerful, the Ultimate Authority, the Always Merciful. Trust in Him alone.

You'll have these wonderful followers. Some family members will become Muslim. But your reliance isn't on them. You don't depend on them for success. You rely on Me, Allah ﷻ says.

### Ayahs 218-220

ٱلَّذِى يَرَىٰكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ • وَتَقَلُّبَكَ فِى ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ • إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

This is a beautiful continuation of the encouragement given earlier in Surah Al-Furqan, where Allah ﷻ describes the believers: وَالَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَامًا - those who spend the night standing and making sajdah for their Lord.

Now the Prophet ﷺ is told: ٱلَّذِى يَرَىٰكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ - rely on the One who sees you when you stand up in the middle of the night to pray. He sees you when you're praying alone, when nobody else can witness your devotion. The One who watches you when no one else can see you - He is the One you should rely on.

وَتَقَلُّبَكَ فِى ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ - and He sees your movement among those who make sajdah. This has multiple beautiful meanings. The Prophet's practice ﷺ was that after finishing his night prayers, he would go out and walk by the houses of the believers, just to see if anyone was awake praying. Perhaps he could hear some Quran being recited. Maybe he could get a glimpse of them making sajdah. He would check on them because he genuinely wanted them to grow spiritually.

So Allah ﷻ says: You should rely on Me, and I know you really want them to grow. I see you when you pray to Me, and I also see you when you go and check on them.

تَقَلُّبَكَ also means "your turning around to see." When someone is leading prayer, can they normally see what's happening behind them? No. But the Prophet ﷺ was miraculously given the ability to see the entire congregation behind him when he led prayers. After finishing, he would sometimes say to someone: "You shouldn't get ahead of me in ruku' and sajdah." The companion would wonder: how did he know? He was in front of us! But he could see - Allah ﷻ had given him this special ability.

So Allah ﷻ says: I see you, just as I have given you the special ability to see the people who make sajdah behind you.

إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - Certainly He, and only He, is the Ultimately Hearing, the All-Knowing. He hears your prayers in the night. He knows your concern for your followers. He sees everything.

## Who the Devils Really Inspire

### Ayahs 221-223

هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن تَنَزَّلُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ • تَنَزَّلُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ • يُلْقُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ كَـٰذِبُونَ

Speaking of devils, Allah ﷻ now addresses their actual influence: هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن تَنَزَّلُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ - should I tell you upon whom the devils actually descend?

تَنَزَّلُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ - they descend upon every perpetual liar who is engrossed in constant sin. أَفَّاكٍ is someone who makes vicious, continuous lies. أَثِيمٍ is one who is always in sin - the sin never stops accumulating.

أَثِيمٍ is also a consequence of being أَفَّاكٍ. When someone keeps spreading lies, those lies spread and multiply even when he's sitting idle.
