Every moment, the consequences of those lies are being propagated, and sin is constantly being accumulated for him. He's called أَثِيمٍ - constantly taking in sin.

That's the person devils descend upon, enabling him to lie at every opportunity he gets.

يُلْقُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ - they throw the hearing, meaning they catch some hearing. They overhear fragments from the angels sometimes. وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ كَـٰذِبُونَ - but most of them are liars. So they might hear something from the angels - a tiny fragment - but then they add lies to it. Fortune tellers operate this way: a grain of stolen truth mixed with fabrications. Most of it is lies, and the people who follow them are also following falsehood.

## The Poets and Their Followers

translation: Who sees you when you arise
And your movement among those who prostrate
Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing
Shall I inform you upon whom the devils descend
They descend upon every sinful liar
They pass on what is heard, and most of them are liars
And the poets - [only] the deviators follow them
commentary:
وَٱلشُّعَرَآءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ ٱلْغَاوُۥنَ

Now the final group is mentioned, giving the Surah its name: وَٱلشُّعَرَآءُ - the poets. This Surah is remarkably poetic, beautifully rhythmic, almost musical in how it flows when recited properly. It's truly incredible.

At the end, Allah ﷻ mentions الشُّعَرَآءُ - the poets. In Arabian society, poets were the philosophical leadership of the Quraysh. Their worldview, their beliefs about life and the afterlife, came from poets. Their understanding of what to love and what to hate came from poets. The poets were their entertainment industry. Higher education in Arabia was essentially poetry. They were the academics, the philosophers, the entertainers - the cultural elite of society.

Every society has different types of elite: political elite, financial elite, social elite. The poets were Arabia's social and cultural elite. They set the standards of culture because they were the ones people went to, listened to, and were influenced by.

When these poets held their performances, did they ask for money? Absolutely. That's how they lived. They fed off two things: the recognition people gave them, and the money people gave them. Poets need an audience that appreciates them, and they need financial compensation.

The entire Surah has been dedicated to explaining that the Prophet ﷺ doesn't have a large audience in Makkah - most of them won't believe, they don't want to hear what he has to say. This stands in direct contrast to poets, who love getting huge audiences. If a poet doesn't find a fan base, he quits. "I tried performing. People threw shoes at me. I give up." But if he had a good audience - a hundred people showed up - he thinks: "I should try again. Maybe I'll get two hundred next time." There's encouragement in numbers.

So the contrast is clear: if the Prophet ﷺ were a poet, and the first ten, fifteen, twenty times the Quraysh weren't interested, he would have quit, just like any poet would. That's what poets do.

And the second thing poets seek, besides audience, is what? Money. What did Allah ﷻ keep repeating in the anthem of all the prophets throughout this Surah? مَا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ - "I don't ask you for any compensation. My compensation is only with the Master of all nations." Not asking for money was emphasized again and again.

So Allah ﷻ mentions poets at the end and says: they don't follow you, Muhammad ﷺ, but they love their poets. And the two things they accuse you of wanting - control over them and their money - are exactly what they know poets actually want. They say: "We will not follow the Prophet ﷺ because he wants money and he wants us to follow him."

Yet the poet explicitly says: "I want your money" and "I want you to follow me" - and you follow him anyway! You're clearly confused. Your criticism of the Prophet ﷺ is the very thing you love about these poets.

الْغَاوُونَ - people who are deeply misled, confused beyond the point of return. Those are the kinds of people who obsessively follow poets.

Think about the connection to our time. The poets were their entertainment industry. When we study this today, we should consider: what is our entertainment industry? Movies, musical artists, hip hop, R&B, rock, pop - and people obsess over them. You hear it on the radio: "You just won tickets!" "I can't believe it! This is the greatest day of my life!" Obsessed.

Allah ﷻ describes something profound about this fan base in one word: الْغَاوُونَ. People who follow entertainers obsessively have no real purpose in life. Their greatest life goal is to meet their idol backstage. That's it. That's all they live for. How lost, how confused, how purposeless.

translation: Do you not see that in every valley they roam
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى كُلِّ وَادٍ يَهِيمُونَ

Now Allah ﷻ describes both the followers and the entertainers themselves: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى كُلِّ وَادٍ يَهِيمُونَ - didn't you observe that they venture into every valley, wandering aimlessly?

يَهِيمُونَ is used for a camel that's lost. A camel became thirsty in the desert, couldn't find water, so it wanders into this valley, then that valley, then another valley - moving aimlessly, desperately, without direction. That's when this word is used.

Allah ﷻ says: didn't you notice that these poets venture off into any valley aimlessly? This means they go into any crowd, trying to please anyone. They pursue any subject matter, attempting to entertain. They make a piece of poetry - it wasn't impressive, people didn't like it. Everything an actor, artist, or singer does is for their audience. If the customers didn't like the product, they have to change it.

So they change their style, their music, their lyrics: "Do you like this better? Is this better?" The audience says, "Yeah, that's pretty good." Then later: "No, this is boring now." So they have to figure out another way to entertain. They venture into this topic and that topic, constantly trying new things to maintain attention.

As they keep trying to entertain, they realize: I'm running out of talent. So they compensate by doing the most obscene, lewd, disgusting things to get attention. "I can't get attention from my talent anymore, so I might as well get arrested for drug abuse, or adopt a baby, or shave my head - at least I'll be mentioned in some magazine at the grocery checkout if not at the award ceremony." They're obsessed with being mentioned, desperate not to be forgotten, willing to humiliate themselves in any way.

They descend into valleys. The imagery of valleys is crucial here. Why mention the valley specifically? Because you're going down - humiliating yourself. Going down is the image of self-humiliation. It also means descending in rank, in dignity.

Consider: when do you have more stability - climbing up a mountain or going down? Going up gives you more control. Going down is far more dangerous. You're descending into danger. When you're up high, you can see clearly. You don't know what's down in the valley - what kind of animals, what kind of dangers lurk there. You don't even know if there's a way back up.

Making your way down, gravity helps you. But making your way back up? You have no easy way out. These people humiliate themselves, degrade themselves, and they can never find their way back up.

I mentioned earlier that poets were the philosophers of that time. In our time, consider people who obsess over philosophy, reading various philosophies for entertainment. One day they're into this philosophy, the next day that one. They descend into these intellectual valleys, and sometimes they can't come out. There's no way to bring them back up. They just stay down there, lost.

Look at the beautiful contrast with the messengers throughout this Surah. Poets keep changing their subject, their genre, their material, their philosophy, constantly trying to keep audiences entertained. But prophets, across all generations, have the exact same anthem - the same words repeated over and over.

Someone reading this Surah might complain: "Why does it keep repeating? Why can't it just say 'they said what was mentioned before' instead of repeating everything?" It's hammering that point deliberately. At the end, it says: here are the people who keep changing their message - those are the entertainers. Prophets aren't here to entertain you. They were given a clear, direct message. They didn't wander this way or that way. They weren't lost like camels. They had a clear sense of direction: the straight path. The same speech, unwavering.

translation: And that they say what they do not do
commentary:
وَأَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ مَا لَا يَفْعَلُونَ

And they say what they don't do - they contradict themselves constantly. Artists contradict themselves all the time. Their words don't match their actions. They say one thing and do another, preach values they don't live by.

translation: Except those [poets] who believe and do righteous deeds and remember Allah often and defend [the Muslims] after they were wronged. And those who have wronged are going to know to what [kind of] return they will be returned
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱنتَصَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ ۗ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَىَّ مُنقَلَبٍ يَنقَلِبُونَ

But then comes the crucial exception: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ - except those who believed and did righteous deeds. Allah ﷻ isn't saying all poets are evil, all entertainers are corrupt, all artists are misguided. There are those among them who believe and do the right thing.

I should mention something powerful here. Who were the only people in society who immediately recognized that what Musa (AS) did wasn't magic? The magicians themselves. They knew their craft and instantly recognized: this is something beyond magic. Similarly, who are the only people in society who will first recognize that this isn't poetry? The poets themselves. They know poetry intimately, and they can tell: this isn't poetry at all. We know our craft, and this transcends it.

So when poets come to Islam, just as the magicians came to faith with Musa (AS), they become among the strongest Muslims - because they recognized the truth through their expertise.

وَذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا - and they remembered Allah ﷻ abundantly. This is key: not just believing, but constantly remembering Allah ﷻ, keeping Him at the center of their consciousness and their art.

وَٱنتَصَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ - and they aided themselves, they helped themselves, after they had been wronged. This phrase carries a subtle but important message. It means they eventually retaliated, even though the current policy for Muslims in Makkah is that you cannot retaliate yet.

This is alluding to the fact that very soon - very soon - there will come a time for legitimate retaliation when the Muslims establish themselves in Medina. Just be patient for now. That time is coming.

وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَىَّ مُنقَلَبٍ يَنقَلِبُونَ - and soon the wrongdoers, the evildoers, are going to find out what final place of return they're going back to.

There's a powerful image here. People come to a concert, listen to a poet, get entertained, then head back home. There's this scene of leaving home to hear the poet, then returning home afterward. Allah ﷻ says: soon you'll discover which real home, which real place of return, you're actually going back to. The wrongdoers will soon find out their ultimate destination.

With that profound warning and promise, this beautiful Surah comes to an end.
